label: Erythema Multiforme
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of widespread skin eruption. He had a cold sore two weeks ago and was otherwise well. The image shows symmetrical target lesions on his palms and forearms, with concentric rings of erythema and central dusky discolouration. There is no mucosal involvement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Erythema multiforme minor
Urticaria
Fixed drug eruption
Pityriasis rosea
correctAnswerText: Erythema multiforme minor
explanation:
This 28-year-old presents with target lesions on the palms and forearms following herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection — the classic trigger for erythema multiforme (EM). The concentric ring "target" morphology is pathognomonic.

Option B (Erythema multiforme minor) is CORRECT because this presentation is classic: target lesions post-HSV, acral distribution (palms/forearms), no mucosal involvement, and no systemic features. EM minor is the most common form, almost always triggered by HSV recurrence, and is self-limiting over 2–4 weeks. Oral aciclovir suppression reduces recurrent EM in HSV-triggered cases per NICE CKS.

Option A (Stevens-Johnson syndrome) is INCORRECT because SJS requires >30% mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital) and epidermal detachment. It is triggered by drugs (sulphonamides, NSAIDs, allopurinol), not typically HSV, and carries significant mortality.

Option C (Urticaria) is INCORRECT because urticarial wheals are raised, pruritic, and transient (individual lesions <24 hours). The fixed, non-pruritic target morphology with central darkening is not urticaria.

Option D (Fixed drug eruption) is INCORRECT because fixed drug eruption causes solitary or few hyperpigmented oval plaques that recur at the same site with drug re-exposure. The bilateral acral symmetry with target morphology excludes this.

Option E (Pityriasis rosea) is INCORRECT because pityriasis rosea shows a herald patch followed by a "Christmas tree" pattern on the trunk, not target lesions on the palms.

label: Psoriatic Arthritis
stem: A 42-year-old man is referred to rheumatology with a 6-month history of painful swollen fingers, morning stiffness lasting over 90 minutes, and asymmetric joint involvement. He has had a rash on his elbows since his twenties. The image shows well-demarcated, silvery-scaled erythematous plaques. He reports that his fingernails have pitting and onycholysis. Rheumatoid factor is negative. What investigation is most appropriate to confirm the likely diagnosis?
options: Anti-CCP antibodies
MRI of the hands
Plain X-rays of hands and feet, and a full clinical assessment using CASPAR criteria
Skin biopsy of the plaque lesion
Anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA)
correctAnswerText: Plain X-rays of hands and feet, and a full clinical assessment using CASPAR criteria
explanation:
This 42-year-old has psoriatic arthritis (PsA): asymmetric inflammatory arthritis, plaque psoriasis, nail pitting, and seronegative (RF-negative) status. The CASPAR (Classification Criteria for Psoriatic Arthritis) score ≥3 confirms diagnosis.

Option C (Plain X-rays hands/feet + CASPAR criteria) is CORRECT. Plain X-rays may show "pencil-in-cup" deformity or periosteal new bone formation. The CASPAR criteria include: current psoriasis (+2), personal/family history of psoriasis (+1), psoriatic nail dystrophy (+1), negative rheumatoid factor (+1), dactylitis (+1), radiological new bone formation (+1). A score ≥3 is required. This structured approach is the recommended diagnostic pathway.

Option A (Anti-CCP antibodies) is INCORRECT because anti-CCP antibodies confirm rheumatoid arthritis (RA), not PsA. PsA is seronegative — anti-CCP is typically negative. The asymmetric pattern and psoriasis make RA unlikely.

Option B (MRI of hands) is INCORRECT as the first-line investigation. While MRI may detect enthesitis and early erosions, clinical assessment with plain radiographs is recommended initially per NICE NG65 before considering MRI.

Option D (Skin biopsy) is INCORRECT because the plaque psoriasis is already clinically evident and histological confirmation of skin disease is unnecessary for the arthritis diagnosis.

Option E (ANA) is INCORRECT because ANA screens for connective tissue diseases such as SLE and Sjögren's syndrome. It is not diagnostic for PsA.

label: Peutz Jeghers Syndrome
stem: A 24-year-old woman attends her GP with recurrent episodes of colicky abdominal pain and a previous episode of intussusception requiring surgery. Her mother had similar episodes and died of bowel cancer aged 52. The image shows multiple small, dark brown, flat macules scattered around the lips and on the buccal mucosa. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Gardner syndrome
Cowden syndrome
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
Familial adenomatous polyposis
HNPCC (Lynch syndrome)
correctAnswerText: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
explanation:
This 24-year-old presents with the classic triad of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS): mucocutaneous pigmentation, hamartomatous GI polyps causing intussusception, and autosomal dominant family history.

Option C (Peutz-Jeghers syndrome) is CORRECT because PJS is caused by STK11 (LKB1) germline mutations. The pathognomonic finding is perioral and mucosal freckling (melanin macules). Hamartomatous polyps cause recurrent intussusception. PJS carries a significantly elevated lifetime risk of GI, breast, pancreatic, ovarian, and cervical cancers. Surveillance colonoscopy and upper GI endoscopy from age 8 are recommended.

Option A (Gardner syndrome) is INCORRECT because Gardner syndrome is a variant of FAP characterised by colorectal adenomatous polyps plus extraintestinal features (osteomas, desmoid tumours, dental anomalies). It does not cause mucocutaneous pigmentation or intussusception.

Option B (Cowden syndrome) is INCORRECT because Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma) presents with trichilemmomas, acral keratoses, and increased risk of breast, thyroid, and endometrial cancer, but without the characteristic perioral pigmentation.

Option D (FAP) is INCORRECT because FAP causes hundreds of colorectal adenomas and desmoid tumours, but not perioral pigmentation or hamartomatous polyps with intussusception.

Option E (Lynch syndrome) is INCORRECT because Lynch syndrome causes microsatellite-unstable colorectal and endometrial cancers without mucocutaneous pigmentation or hamartomatous polyps.

label: Measles
stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought by his mother with a 3-day history of fever, runny nose, cough, and red eyes. He is unvaccinated. The image shows multiple small, white spots on an erythematous base, distributed across the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars. A widespread maculopapular rash has begun spreading from behind the ears. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Scarlet fever
Kawasaki disease
Measles (rubeola)
Roseola infantum
Hand, foot and mouth disease
correctAnswerText: Measles (rubeola)
explanation:
This unvaccinated 4-year-old has measles: the classic prodrome of the three Cs (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis), fever, and the pathognomonic Koplik spots shown in the image — small white spots on erythematous buccal mucosa. The maculopapular rash spreading cephalocaudally confirms measles.

Option C (Measles) is CORRECT because Koplik spots appear 1–2 days before the rash and are specific to measles caused by measles morbillivirus. The rash begins behind the ears and spreads down the body. Measles is a notifiable disease in the UK. Management is supportive; vitamin A supplementation reduces complications in deficient children. Contact with immunocompromised individuals and non-immune pregnant women must be prevented.

Option A (Scarlet fever) is INCORRECT because scarlet fever (group A Streptococcus) produces a fine sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and circumoral pallor. It does not cause Koplik spots or conjunctivitis.

Option B (Kawasaki disease) is INCORRECT because Kawasaki disease requires ≥5 days of fever plus ≥4 of five features: rash, conjunctivitis, lymphadenopathy, lip changes, and hand/foot oedema. Koplik spots are not a feature.

Option D (Roseola infantum) is INCORRECT because roseola (HHV-6) causes high fever followed by a fine pink rash as the fever resolves — there is no prodrome of the three Cs and no Koplik spots.

Option E (Hand, foot and mouth disease) is INCORRECT because HFMD causes vesicles on the palms, soles, and oral mucosa, not the white spots on erythematous buccal mucosa that characterise Koplik spots.

label: Iron Deficiency Anaemia
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue and exertional breathlessness for 4 months. She has heavy menstrual periods. The image shows her nails, which are thin, brittle, and spoon-shaped (concave). Blood tests: Hb 82 g/L, MCV 68 fL, serum ferritin 6 mcg/L. What is the most appropriate initial investigation to determine the cause of her anaemia before starting treatment?
options: Bone marrow biopsy
Reticulocyte count
Upper GI endoscopy and colonoscopy
Haematinics including B12, folate, and ferritin
Coeliac serology (anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA)
correctAnswerText: Coeliac serology (anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA)
explanation:
The nail finding shown — koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) — combined with microcytic hypochromic anaemia and low ferritin confirms iron deficiency anaemia (IDA). In a 35-year-old woman with menorrhagia, the most appropriate investigation to identify a co-existing or alternative cause before treatment is coeliac serology.

Option E (Coeliac serology) is CORRECT because NICE NG20 recommends that all patients with IDA of unclear aetiology should have anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (anti-tTG) measured. Coeliac disease causes malabsorption of iron and is a frequently missed cause of IDA, particularly in women of reproductive age. Early identification changes management fundamentally — a gluten-free diet rather than supplementation alone.

Option A (Bone marrow biopsy) is INCORRECT because bone marrow examination is not indicated in straightforward IDA with a clear clinical picture and low ferritin. It is reserved for unexplained cytopenias or suspected haematological malignancy.

Option B (Reticulocyte count) is INCORRECT as the priority investigation. Reticulocytes are useful to assess erythropoietic response to treatment but do not determine the cause of IDA.

Option C (Upper GI endoscopy/colonoscopy) is INCORRECT as the immediate first investigation. Endoscopy to exclude GI blood loss is appropriate — but in a premenopausal woman with heavy periods and no GI symptoms, menorrhagia is the most likely cause. Coeliac testing is prioritised first per NICE.

Option D (Haematinics including B12 and folate) is INCORRECT because the diagnosis of iron deficiency is already confirmed by low ferritin and microcytosis. B12 and folate testing would be relevant for macrocytic or normocytic anaemia.

label: Polymorphic Eruption Of Pregnancy
stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation presents with intensely itchy papules and urticarial plaques that began within the abdominal striae and are spreading to the thighs. The image shows erythematous urticarial plaques confined to the abdominal striae with periumbilical sparing. Liver function tests, bile acids, and urine dipstick are all normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Obstetric cholestasis (intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy)
Pemphigoid gestationis (herpes gestationis)
Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP)
Atopic eruption of pregnancy
Psoriasis exacerbated by pregnancy
correctAnswerText: Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP)
explanation:
This 36-week primigravida has the classic presentation of polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP), also called pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy (PUPPP). Key features: first pregnancy, onset within the abdominal striae in the third trimester, periumbilical sparing, normal liver function and bile acids.

Option C (PEP) is CORRECT because PEP is the most common specific dermatosis of pregnancy, occurring in approximately 1 in 160 pregnancies, almost always in primigravidas and multiple gestations. It begins within striae and shows characteristic periumbilical sparing (distinguishing it from pemphigoid gestationis). Normal bile acids exclude obstetric cholestasis. Management is emollients, topical corticosteroids (mild-moderate for symptom relief), and antihistamines. It resolves spontaneously after delivery without fetal risk.

Option A (Obstetric cholestasis) is INCORRECT because intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) causes pruritus without a primary rash (scratch marks may be seen) with elevated bile acids >10 μmol/L and/or abnormal LFTs. Normal bile acids exclude this diagnosis.

Option B (Pemphigoid gestationis) is INCORRECT because pemphigoid gestationis is an autoimmune blistering disorder associated with anti-BP180 antibodies. It begins in the periumbilical area and progresses to bullae — the periumbilical sparing here and absence of blisters make this unlikely.

Option D (Atopic eruption of pregnancy) is INCORRECT because AEP typically presents earlier in pregnancy in women with a personal or family history of atopy as eczematous patches, often on flexures.

Option E (Psoriasis) is INCORRECT because psoriasis typically improves in pregnancy due to immune changes. New-onset widespread psoriasis in pregnancy without personal history is uncommon.

label: Iga Vasculitis
stem: A 7-year-old boy presents following an upper respiratory tract infection one week ago. The image shows symmetrical palpable purpura predominantly on the buttocks and lower limbs, sparing the trunk. He also has mild arthralgia of the ankles and bilateral knee joints. Urinalysis shows haematuria and proteinuria. Blood tests: platelet count 320 × 10⁹/L, coagulation screen normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate platelet transfusion
Skin biopsy and request IgA level
Referral to paediatric nephrology for urinalysis monitoring, and blood pressure measurement
Start prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day immediately
Bone marrow aspiration to exclude leukaemia
correctAnswerText: Referral to paediatric nephrology for urinalysis monitoring, and blood pressure measurement
explanation:
This 7-year-old has IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura — HSP): palpable purpura on buttocks and lower limbs with normal platelets, post-URTI onset, arthralgia, and haematuria/proteinuria indicating HSP nephritis. The most important immediate step is monitoring for renal involvement.

Option C (Paediatric nephrology referral with urinalysis and BP monitoring) is CORRECT because HSP nephritis (IgA vasculitis nephritis) determines long-term prognosis. NICE CKS and BSPED recommend regular urinalysis for at least 6 months after diagnosis. Blood pressure monitoring detects hypertensive nephropathy early. Nephrology referral is indicated when there is significant proteinuria or progressive renal impairment.

Option A (Platelet transfusion) is INCORRECT because platelet count is normal (320 × 10⁹/L) — this confirms the purpura is palpable (vasculitic), not thrombocytopaenic. Thrombocytopaenic purpura (ITP) causes non-palpable purpura with low platelets.

Option B (Skin biopsy and IgA level) is INCORRECT as the immediate next step. While skin biopsy showing IgA deposition confirms HSP, it is not required when the clinical diagnosis is clear. Serum IgA level is elevated in only 50% of cases and does not change management.

Option D (Start prednisolone immediately) is INCORRECT because steroids are not recommended for uncomplicated HSP. They may be used for severe abdominal pain or significant nephritis but are not given routinely without assessment of severity.

Option E (Bone marrow aspiration) is INCORRECT because normal platelets and symmetrical purpura in a post-URTI context make leukaemia very unlikely. Bone marrow examination is reserved for unexplained cytopenias with blasts.

label: Sarcoidosis
stem: A 32-year-old Black woman presents with a 2-month history of dry cough, mild breathlessness on exertion, and bilateral ankle swelling. She is a non-smoker. The image shows symmetrical bilateral hilar enlargement with a lobulated contour and no lung parenchymal shadowing. Serum calcium is 2.78 mmol/L. Serum ACE is elevated. Spirometry shows a mildly restrictive pattern. What does this chest X-ray most likely demonstrate?
options: Bilateral pulmonary metastases
Primary tuberculosis (Ghon complex)
Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy consistent with stage I sarcoidosis
Congestive cardiac failure with bilateral pleural effusions
Malignant lymphoma (Hodgkin lymphoma)
correctAnswerText: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy consistent with stage I sarcoidosis
explanation:
The CXR shows symmetrical bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) without pulmonary infiltrates — this is stage I sarcoidosis. The combination of BHL, elevated ACE, hypercalcaemia, and the demographic (young Black woman) is classic for sarcoidosis.

Option C (Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy — stage I sarcoidosis) is CORRECT. Sarcoidosis staging by CXR: Stage I = BHL only; Stage II = BHL + parenchymal infiltrates; Stage III = parenchymal infiltrates only; Stage IV = fibrosis. The symmetrical "lobulated" hilar appearance is pathognomonic. Elevated ACE reflects granuloma burden. Hypercalcaemia results from activated macrophage production of 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. Stage I sarcoidosis has spontaneous resolution in 60–80% of cases.

Option A (Bilateral pulmonary metastases) is INCORRECT because metastases produce pulmonary nodules, not smooth bilateral hilar enlargement. The hilar lobulation here is lymphadenopathy, not vascular enlargement.

Option B (Primary TB) is INCORRECT because primary TB typically causes unilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with a Ghon focus, not symmetrical BHL. TB should be considered but is less likely given the symmetric appearance and elevated ACE.

Option D (Congestive cardiac failure) is INCORRECT because CCF shows cardiomegaly, upper lobe venous diversion, Kerley B lines, and bilateral basal effusions — not symmetrical lobulated hilar enlargement.

Option E (Hodgkin lymphoma) is INCORRECT because while HL can cause BHL, it is typically asymmetric and associated with constitutional B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss). Elevated ACE is specific to sarcoidosis.

label: Acute Pulmonary Oedema
stem: An 80-year-old man with known ischaemic heart disease is brought to A&E with 2-hour history of severe breathlessness and frothy pink sputum. His oxygen saturation is 84% on air. The image shows bilateral perihilar "bat-wing" infiltrates, cardiomegaly, upper lobe vascular diversion, and bilateral small pleural effusions. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start IV furosemide 40 mg, high-flow oxygen (target SpO₂ 94–98%), sit patient upright, and arrange urgent echocardiogram
Immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation
IV amiodarone 300 mg loading dose
Chest physiotherapy and salbutamol nebuliser
Urgent CT pulmonary angiography to exclude PE
correctAnswerText: Start IV furosemide 40 mg, high-flow oxygen (target SpO₂ 94–98%), sit patient upright, and arrange urgent echocardiogram
explanation:
This 80-year-old with ischaemic heart disease presents with acute pulmonary oedema: frothy pink sputum, severe hypoxia, and the classic CXR features — bilateral "bat-wing" perihilar infiltrates, cardiomegaly, upper lobe venous diversion, and pleural effusions.

Option A (IV furosemide 40 mg, high-flow O₂, sit upright, urgent echo) is CORRECT per NICE NG106. Immediate management includes: sitting the patient upright (reduces cardiac preload), high-flow oxygen (target SpO₂ 94–98%), IV loop diuretic (furosemide 40 mg to promote diuresis and venodilation), and urgent echocardiogram to identify the underlying cause (e.g., MI, severe mitral regurgitation). Consider IV nitrates if BP allows.

Option B (Immediate intubation) is INCORRECT as the first-line intervention. Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) should be trialled before invasive ventilation in acute pulmonary oedema if the patient can protect their airway. SpO₂ of 84% is severe but not immediately imminently fatal with oxygen.

Option C (IV amiodarone) is INCORRECT because amiodarone is used for arrhythmias (VT, AF). There is no indication of arrhythmia in this scenario. Amiodarone does not treat pulmonary oedema.

Option D (Chest physiotherapy and salbutamol) is INCORRECT because this is cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, not obstructive airways disease. Salbutamol is not indicated and may cause tachycardia worsening cardiac workload.

Option E (CT pulmonary angiography) is INCORRECT because the clinical and radiological picture is unambiguously acute pulmonary oedema, not PE. CTPA requires patient transport and contrast injection — delay would be dangerous.

label: Perforated Viscus
stem: A 56-year-old man with a history of peptic ulcer disease presents with sudden onset severe generalised abdominal pain that began 3 hours ago. On examination he has generalised peritonism, absent bowel sounds, and board-like abdominal rigidity. The image shows a crescent of gas under both hemidiaphragms. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent upper GI endoscopy to identify the bleeding source
CT abdomen with IV contrast
Nil by mouth, IV access, IV antibiotics, analgesia, urgent surgical referral, and consent for laparotomy
Nasogastric tube insertion and observation for 6 hours
IV omeprazole 40 mg twice daily and Helicobacter pylori eradication
correctAnswerText: Nil by mouth, IV access, IV antibiotics, analgesia, urgent surgical referral, and consent for laparotomy
explanation:
This man has a perforated peptic ulcer: classic history of peptic ulcer disease, sudden severe abdominal pain, peritonism, and the pathognomonic finding of free gas under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on erect CXR.

Option C (Nil by mouth, IV access, IV antibiotics, analgesia, urgent surgical referral, consent for laparotomy) is CORRECT. This is a surgical emergency. Immediate management: NBM, IV access, fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam), adequate analgesia, urinary catheter, and urgent surgical referral. Laparotomy for peritoneal washout and perforation repair (Graham patch) is the definitive treatment. Delay increases mortality significantly.

Option A (Upper GI endoscopy) is INCORRECT because endoscopy is contraindicated in the context of perforation and pneumoperitoneum. It may worsen the perforation and delay definitive surgical treatment.

Option B (CT abdomen) is INCORRECT as the immediate management step. CT may be performed to characterise the perforation if the diagnosis is unclear, but when clinical signs of peritonitis and pneumoperitoneum are present, the priority is immediate surgical referral, not further imaging.

Option D (NGT insertion and observation) is INCORRECT because free peritoneal gas with peritonitis constitutes a surgical emergency. Observation would cause preventable deterioration and death from sepsis.

Option E (IV omeprazole and H. pylori eradication) is INCORRECT as the immediate treatment. These are appropriate for managing non-perforated peptic ulcer disease but are inadequate for an acute perforation requiring surgical repair.

label: Subdural Haematoma
stem: A 78-year-old woman on warfarin (for AF, INR 2.8) is brought to A&E following a fall 3 days ago. She has been increasingly confused and her family reports that she has not been her usual self. GCS is 13 (E3, V4, M6). The CT head shows bilateral crescent-shaped hyperdense collections between the brain surface and the dura, causing midline shift. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: MRI brain with gadolinium contrast
Immediate reversal of anticoagulation with Vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC), urgent neurosurgical referral
IV dexamethasone 8 mg to reduce cerebral oedema
Lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage
Observation and repeat CT in 24 hours
correctAnswerText: Immediate reversal of anticoagulation with Vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC), urgent neurosurgical referral
explanation:
This 78-year-old anticoagulated patient has bilateral acute-on-chronic subdural haematomas (SDH): crescent-shaped collections with midline shift, recent fall history, and progressive confusion. Anticoagulation reversal is the immediate priority.

Option B (Immediate anticoagulation reversal with Vitamin K + PCC, urgent neurosurgical referral) is CORRECT. With INR 2.8 and an intracranial haematoma, warfarin must be reversed immediately. PCC provides rapid reversal (within minutes), while IV Vitamin K 5–10 mg ensures sustained reversal. The midline shift and GCS depression indicate likely need for burr-hole evacuation — urgent neurosurgical referral is essential. NICE NG68 mandates immediate reversal in ICH on anticoagulants.

Option A (MRI with gadolinium) is INCORRECT because CT head has already confirmed the diagnosis. MRI provides no additional immediate benefit and causes a dangerous delay in anticoagulation reversal and surgical intervention.

Option C (IV dexamethasone) is INCORRECT because dexamethasone is not indicated in traumatic intracranial haematoma. Steroids may worsen outcomes in traumatic brain injury and are only used in cerebral oedema secondary to tumour.

Option D (Lumbar puncture) is INCORRECT because LP is absolutely contraindicated when there is raised intracranial pressure with midline shift, as it risks fatal transtentorial herniation.

Option E (Observation and repeat CT) is INCORRECT because deteriorating GCS, midline shift, and anticoagulant use constitute urgent indications for reversal and neurosurgical intervention, not watchful waiting.

label: Pagets Disease
stem: A 74-year-old retired builder attends orthopaedics with increasing hat size over several years, frontal headache, and bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is 890 IU/L; calcium, phosphate, and PTH are all normal. The image shows an enlarged skull vault with a "cotton-wool" appearance — areas of sclerosis interspersed with lucency — and thickening of the outer table. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Osteomalacia
Hyperparathyroidism (osteitis fibrosa cystica)
Multiple myeloma
Paget's disease of bone (osteitis deformans)
Skull metastases from prostate cancer
correctAnswerText: Paget's disease of bone (osteitis deformans)
explanation:
This 74-year-old has Paget's disease of bone: classic skull features on X-ray (cotton-wool sclerosis, vault thickening), isolated markedly elevated ALP with normal calcium/phosphate, increasing hat size, deafness (VIII nerve compression), and headache (skull base compression).

Option D (Paget's disease) is CORRECT because the cotton-wool radiological appearance of the skull is pathognomonic for Paget's disease. Paget's is characterised by disorganised osteoclast-mediated bone resorption followed by excessive osteoblastic activity, producing structurally weak, enlarged bone. ALP reflects osteoblastic activity and is markedly elevated while calcium, phosphate, and PTH remain normal. Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) are first-line treatment per NICE.

Option A (Osteomalacia) is INCORRECT because osteomalacia causes diffuse bone pain, hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, and elevated ALP with Looser zones (pseudofractures) on X-ray — not the cotton-wool pattern. Calcium is normal here.

Option B (Hyperparathyroidism) is INCORRECT because primary hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcaemia and a 'salt-and-pepper' skull appearance on X-ray, not cotton-wool densities. PTH is elevated in hyperparathyroidism, which is normal here.

Option C (Multiple myeloma) is INCORRECT because myeloma causes 'punched-out' lytic lesions in the skull (not sclerotic cotton-wool), elevated ESR/paraprotein, and renal impairment. ALP is typically normal in myeloma (no osteoblastic activity).

Option E (Skull metastases from prostate cancer) is INCORRECT because prostate metastases are sclerotic (not mixed sclerotic/lytic) and would be associated with elevated PSA, not isolated elevated ALP with normal calcium.

label: Acromegaly
stem: A 44-year-old man is referred with a 5-year history of increasing shoe and glove size, prominence of the jaw, excessive sweating, and headache. His wife has noticed facial changes in photographs over the years. The image shows an enlarged pituitary fossa (expanded sella turcica), frontal bossing, and prognathism on the lateral skull X-ray. IGF-1 is markedly elevated. What does this lateral skull X-ray most likely demonstrate?
options: Pituitary apoplexy
Craniopharyngioma with suprasellar calcification
Radiological features consistent with acromegaly — enlarged sella turcica with prognathism
Paget's disease of the skull
Normal variant — large sella turcica
correctAnswerText: Radiological features consistent with acromegaly — enlarged sella turcica with prognathism
explanation:
The lateral skull X-ray shows the classic features of acromegaly caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma: enlarged sella turcica (expanded pituitary fossa), frontal bossing (thickened skull vault), and prognathism (protruding mandible). Combined with elevated IGF-1, this confirms acromegaly.

Option C (Acromegaly — enlarged sella turcica with prognathism) is CORRECT because chronic GH excess causes bony enlargement through periosteal new bone formation. The sella enlarges as the pituitary tumour grows. IGF-1 is the best screening test for acromegaly (reflects GH action); confirmation is by failure of GH suppression during oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). Treatment is transsphenoidal surgery as first-line per NICE.

Option A (Pituitary apoplexy) is INCORRECT because apoplexy refers to acute haemorrhage or infarction into a pituitary tumour, causing sudden severe headache, visual field defects, and meningism. It does not cause the chronic bony changes seen here.

Option B (Craniopharyngioma) is INCORRECT because craniopharyngioma typically occurs in children/young adults and shows suprasellar calcification on X-ray. IGF-1 would be low, not elevated.

Option D (Paget's disease) is INCORRECT because Paget's causes cotton-wool sclerosis of the skull vault, not selective enlargement of the sella turcica with prognathism.

Option E (Normal variant) is INCORRECT because the combination of markedly enlarged sella with prognathism and elevated IGF-1 is not a normal finding. Clinically, the shoe/ring size increase, sweating, and headache confirm active acromegaly.

label: Staghorn Calculus
stem: A 48-year-old woman with recurrent urinary tract infections presents with dull left loin pain, nocturia, and foul-smelling urine. Urine culture grows Proteus mirabilis. The KUB X-ray shows a large radio-opaque calculus in the left renal pelvis and calyces that fills and conforms to the pelvicalyceal system. Serum creatinine is 98 μmol/L. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Long-term prophylactic trimethoprim and watchful waiting
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) and antibiotic treatment of underlying infection
Ureteroscopy and laser fragmentation
Increase fluid intake to >3 litres per day and urine alkalinisation
correctAnswerText: Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) and antibiotic treatment of underlying infection
explanation:
This woman has a staghorn calculus (struvite/infection stone) filling the left pelvicalyceal system. Staghorn calculi are caused by urease-producing organisms (Proteus, Klebsiella) that split urea to ammonia, alkalinising the urine and precipitating struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate).

Option C (PCNL and antibiotic treatment) is CORRECT because PCNL is the treatment of choice for large staghorn calculi per NICE guidance and EAU guidelines. PCNL involves percutaneous access to the kidney to fragment and remove stones under direct vision. It achieves the highest stone-free rates for complex/staghorn stones. Concurrent treatment of Proteus UTI with appropriate antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav or ciprofloxacin based on sensitivity) is essential to prevent recurrence and septic complications.

Option A (Trimethoprim and watchful waiting) is INCORRECT because staghorn calculi do not resolve without intervention and untreated infection stones lead to renal destruction, xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis, and sepsis. Antibiotic suppression alone is inadequate.

Option B (ESWL) is INCORRECT because ESWL is contraindicated for large staghorn calculi (>20 mm). Fragment clearance is very poor, and fragments may cause ureteric obstruction ("steinstrasse"). PCNL is preferred.

Option D (Ureteroscopy and laser) is INCORRECT because ureteroscopy is suitable for ureteric calculi and smaller renal stones, not large staghorn calculi filling the entire pelvicalyceal system.

Option E (Increased fluids and alkalinisation) is INCORRECT because this may help prevent calcium oxalate stones. Struvite stones require alkaline urine (they form in alkaline urine) and further alkalinisation would promote their growth rather than dissolution.

label: Wpw Syndrome
stem: A 23-year-old man presents with recurrent episodes of palpitations and one episode of syncope. He has no structural heart disease on echocardiogram. The 12-lead ECG is shown. The trace shows a short PR interval (<120 ms), a slurred upstroke of the QRS complex (delta wave), and broad QRS complexes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Long QT syndrome type 1
Brugada syndrome
Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome
Right bundle branch block with first-degree AV block
correctAnswerText: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
explanation:
This 23-year-old has the ECG hallmarks of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome: short PR interval (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred initial QRS upstroke), and broad QRS — the "WPW triad."

Option C (Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome) is CORRECT. WPW results from an accessory conduction pathway (Bundle of Kent) that bypasses the AV node, causing ventricular pre-excitation. Pre-excitation creates the delta wave. Patients are at risk of AVRT (orthodromic or antidromic re-entry tachycardia) and, critically, rapid ventricular response in AF via the accessory pathway — which can degenerate to VF. Management includes electrophysiological study and radiofrequency catheter ablation of the accessory pathway.

Option A (Long QT syndrome) is INCORRECT because LQTS shows a prolonged QTc interval with normal PR interval and no delta wave. It predisposes to torsades de pointes, not pre-excitation.

Option B (Brugada syndrome) is INCORRECT because Brugada shows a right bundle branch block-like pattern with coved ST elevation in V1-V3. PR interval and QRS are not characterised by pre-excitation or delta wave.

Option D (Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome) is INCORRECT because LGL shows a short PR interval WITHOUT a delta wave or broad QRS — the conduction travels through the AV node via a James fibre, not a Kent bundle.

Option E (RBBB with first-degree AV block) is INCORRECT because RBBB causes a prolonged (not shortened) PR and RSR' pattern in V1 with wide S in V6. There is no delta wave in RBBB.

label: Pericarditis
stem: A 29-year-old man presents with sharp central chest pain that worsens on lying flat and improves on leaning forward. He had a viral illness 5 days ago. The ECG shown demonstrates widespread concave ("saddle-shaped") ST elevation in most leads with PR depression in I, II, V4-V6 and PR elevation in aVR. Troponin is mildly elevated. Echocardiogram shows a small pericardial effusion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Anterior STEMI requiring primary PCI
Hyperkalaemia
Acute pericarditis
Left ventricular aneurysm
Benign early repolarisation
correctAnswerText: Acute pericarditis
explanation:
This 29-year-old has acute pericarditis: viral prodrome, positional pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward (reduced friction on pericardium), widespread saddle-shaped ST elevation, and PR depression — the classic ECG pattern.

Option C (Acute pericarditis) is CORRECT. The four ECG stages of pericarditis: Stage 1 — widespread concave ST elevation and PR depression (this patient); Stage 2 — ST returns to baseline, PR remains depressed; Stage 3 — T-wave inversions; Stage 4 — ECG normalises. PR depression is due to atrial inflammation (epicardial current vector). Management per ESC guidelines: aspirin 500–1000 mg three times daily or ibuprofen 400–600 mg three times daily plus colchicine 500 mcg twice daily for 3 months to reduce recurrence. Restrict strenuous activity for ≥3 months.

Option A (Anterior STEMI) is INCORRECT because STEMI shows focal, convex ST elevation in a coronary territory, not widespread concave elevation across multiple leads. Reciprocal ST depression would be seen in STEMI. This patient's young age, viral prodrome, and positional relief make STEMI very unlikely.

Option B (Hyperkalaemia) is INCORRECT because hyperkalaemia causes peaked T waves, widened QRS, and loss of P waves — not widespread saddle-shaped ST elevation with PR depression.

Option D (LV aneurysm) is INCORRECT because LV aneurysm causes persistent ST elevation (typically anterior leads V1-V4) with deep Q waves after a previous MI. The widespread distribution and PR depression are not features of LV aneurysm.

Option E (Benign early repolarisation) is INCORRECT because BER shows focal ST elevation in V2-V5 in young men with "fish-hook" J-point notching. BER does not cause PR depression or the widespread saddle-shaped morphology.

label: Right Bundle Branch Block
stem: A 55-year-old man with no cardiac symptoms has an ECG performed as part of a pre-employment medical. The trace shows an RSR' pattern (M-shaped complex) in V1 and V2, wide slurred S waves in leads I, aVL, and V5-V6, and a QRS duration of 140 ms. He is asymptomatic with no history of heart disease. What does this ECG demonstrate?
options: Left bundle branch block — requires urgent cardiac investigation
Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
Right bundle branch block (RBBB) — an incidental finding, no immediate action required
Ventricular tachycardia requiring urgent cardioversion
Anterior STEMI with right ventricular involvement
correctAnswerText: Right bundle branch block (RBBB) — an incidental finding, no immediate action required
explanation:
The ECG shows the classic features of right bundle branch block (RBBB): RSR' pattern in V1-V2 (M-shaped "rabbit ears"), broad slurred S waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6), and QRS >120 ms. In an asymptomatic man with no cardiac history, isolated RBBB is usually benign.

Option C (RBBB — incidental finding, no immediate action) is CORRECT. Isolated RBBB is commonly found incidentally in otherwise healthy individuals and does not require urgent investigation or treatment. The RBBB pattern results from delayed activation of the right ventricle — the left ventricle activates normally, then depolarisation "crosses over" to the right ventricle, creating the broad QRS and RSR' morphology. If RBBB is new and symptomatic (dyspnoea, syncope), investigation for structural heart disease (echo, Holter) is warranted. Asymptomatic isolated RBBB requires no specific management.

Option A (LBBB requiring urgent investigation) is INCORRECT because LBBB (not RBBB) shows broad monophasic R waves in lateral leads and QS or rS in V1. New LBBB with chest pain is treated as STEMI equivalent, but this is RBBB, not LBBB.

Option B (WPW) is INCORRECT because WPW shows a short PR interval and delta wave — initial slurring of the QRS. RBBB shows a slurred terminal portion (S waves laterally, R' in V1) not the initial delta wave.

Option D (VT) is INCORRECT because VT produces a regular wide complex tachycardia typically at >100 bpm without clear P waves. This ECG presumably shows a normal rate with distinct RSR' morphology, not the pattern of VT.

Option E (Anterior STEMI with RV involvement) is INCORRECT because acute STEMI would show ST elevation in a coronary distribution. RBBB does not in itself indicate STEMI, though new RBBB can sometimes accompany large anterior MI (left anterior descending artery territory).

label: Hyperkalaemia
stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3b is admitted with reduced urine output and confusion. He takes ramipril, spironolactone, and metformin. Serum potassium is 7.2 mmol/L, creatinine 380 μmol/L. The ECG shows tall, peaked symmetrical T waves in precordial leads, widening of the QRS complex to 160 ms, and a "sine wave" pattern. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Stop ramipril and spironolactone, and restrict dietary potassium
IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution over 2–5 minutes, followed by insulin-dextrose infusion
Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 mL IV bolus
Calcium resonium (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) rectally stat
Urgent renal biopsy
correctAnswerText: IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution over 2–5 minutes, followed by insulin-dextrose infusion
explanation:
This man has severe hyperkalaemia (K+ 7.2 mmol/L) with life-threatening ECG changes: peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave pattern, which indicates imminent ventricular fibrillation. This is a cardiac emergency requiring immediate membrane stabilisation.

Option B (IV calcium gluconate then insulin-dextrose) is CORRECT. The sequence in severe symptomatic hyperkalaemia is: (1) IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% over 2–5 minutes — stabilises the cardiac membrane within minutes (does not lower K+); (2) Insulin-dextrose (10 units actrapid in 50 mL of 50% dextrose) — drives K+ into cells, lowering serum K+ by 0.5–1.0 mmol/L over 15–30 minutes; (3) Salbutamol 10–20 mg nebulised — further shifts K+ intracellularly; (4) Definitive removal: calcium resonium, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (patiromer), or dialysis.

Option A (Stop ramipril/spironolactone, dietary restriction) is INCORRECT as the immediate action. Stopping the offending agents and dietary restriction are appropriate medium-term measures but have no acute effect on critically elevated K+ with cardiac arrhythmia risk.

Option C (Sodium bicarbonate) is INCORRECT as first-line in hyperkalaemia. Bicarbonate used to be recommended but recent evidence and NICE guidance no longer recommend it as routine first-line treatment for hyperkalaemia. Calcium gluconate is the priority.

Option D (Calcium resonium rectally) is INCORRECT as the immediate step. Ion exchange resins act over hours and are used for medium-term K+ removal after initial emergency management, not as the first-line cardiac membrane-stabilising agent.

Option E (Renal biopsy) is INCORRECT because there is no indication for biopsy in acute-on-chronic renal failure from medication-induced hyperkalaemia without glomerulonephritis features.

label: Long Qt Hypothyroidism
stem: A 54-year-old woman with known autoimmune hypothyroidism (not currently treated) presents with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and one episode of syncope. She takes no other medications. The ECG shows a heart rate of 48 bpm, prolonged QTc of 580 ms, and low voltage complexes. TSH is 85 mIU/L, free T4 <5 pmol/L. What is the most appropriate immediate management to address the ECG abnormality?
options: IV amiodarone for rate control
Start levothyroxine cautiously at a low dose (25–50 mcg/day) with regular TSH monitoring — the QT will normalise with thyroid replacement
Immediate cardiac pacing for bradycardia
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) insertion
Magnesium sulfate IV for torsades de pointes prophylaxis
correctAnswerText: Start levothyroxine cautiously at a low dose (25–50 mcg/day) with regular TSH monitoring — the QT will normalise with thyroid replacement
explanation:
This woman has severe hypothyroidism (TSH 85, low free T4) causing bradycardia, prolonged QTc (580 ms), and low-voltage ECG. The prolonged QT in hypothyroidism is secondary to metabolic slowing of cardiac repolarisation and reverses with thyroid hormone replacement.

Option B (Levothyroxine starting at low dose 25–50 mcg/day) is CORRECT because the underlying cause of the QT prolongation is hypothyroidism, not a primary cardiac channelopathy. Levothyroxine replacement gradually corrects the metabolic derangement, normalising heart rate and QTc over weeks to months. In severe hypothyroidism, starting doses should be low (25 mcg in elderly patients, 50 mcg in younger) with gradual dose titration to avoid precipitating angina or arrhythmia in patients with possible coronary disease. TSH monitoring guides dose adjustment.

Option A (IV amiodarone) is INCORRECT because amiodarone itself markedly prolongs the QT interval and blocks T4-to-T3 conversion — it would worsen the situation. Amiodarone is absolutely avoided in patients with QTc >500 ms or hypothyroid state.

Option C (Immediate cardiac pacing) is INCORRECT because the bradycardia and prolonged QT are metabolic (hypothyroid) rather than intrinsic conduction disease. Pacing does not address the cause and is not indicated unless the patient is haemodynamically compromised or symptomatic from the bradycardia alone.

Option D (ICD insertion) is INCORRECT because ICD is reserved for inherited channelopathies (congenital LQTS) with high-risk features or prior aborted cardiac arrest. Secondary causes of QT prolongation do not require ICD if the underlying cause is treated.

Option E (Magnesium sulfate) is INCORRECT as the primary management. Magnesium is used acutely for torsades de pointes, but this patient is not currently in torsades. The priority is to treat the underlying cause.

label: Pulmonary Embolism Ecg
stem: A 38-year-old woman, 6 weeks post-caesarean section, presents with sudden onset pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. She is tachycardic at 115 bpm, SpO₂ 90%. The ECG shown demonstrates sinus tachycardia, new right axis deviation, S wave in lead I, Q wave in lead III, inverted T wave in lead III (S1Q3T3 pattern), and right bundle branch block. D-dimer is 3.8 μg/mL. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block
Right ventricular myocardial infarction
Massive pulmonary embolism causing acute right heart strain
Ventricular tachycardia degenerating from RBBB
Anterior STEMI with right ventricular involvement
correctAnswerText: Massive pulmonary embolism causing acute right heart strain
explanation:
This post-caesarean woman with pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, tachycardia, and the S1Q3T3 ECG pattern plus RBBB has massive pulmonary embolism with acute right heart strain. Post-partum period is one of the highest-risk periods for VTE.

Option C (Massive PE with acute right heart strain) is CORRECT. The S1Q3T3 pattern (though not sensitive) is the classic ECG sign of acute PE due to right ventricular pressure overload. RBBB results from right bundle compression or ischaemia from right heart dilation. The combination of post-partum state, hypoxia, tachycardia, and elevated D-dimer strongly supports massive PE. Management: immediate anticoagulation (LMWH or unfractionated heparin), if massive PE with haemodynamic instability — consider thrombolysis (alteplase) or surgical embolectomy per NICE NG158 and BTS guidelines.

Option A (Atrial flutter with 2:1 block) is INCORRECT because atrial flutter produces regular sawtooth P waves at 300 bpm with ventricular rate of 150 bpm. The patient has sinus tachycardia at 115 bpm, not the characteristic flutter pattern.

Option B (RV myocardial infarction) is INCORRECT because RV MI typically follows inferior STEMI with ST elevation in right-sided leads (V4R, V5R). While RV strain occurs in both, the clinical context (post-partum, high D-dimer, pleuritic pain) and lack of Q waves in inferior leads make PE far more likely.

Option D (VT from RBBB) is INCORRECT because RBBB is not a substrate for VT. The patient is haemodynamically stable enough to be assessed, with a clear clinical context for PE.

Option E (Anterior STEMI) is INCORRECT because there are no ST elevations in anterior leads. The RBBB here is a new finding from right heart strain, not the wide QRS of STEMI.

label: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage
stem: A 51-year-old woman presents with the "worst headache of her life" of sudden onset during exertion. She is drowsy (GCS 13). CT head confirms subarachnoid haemorrhage. The ECG performed on admission shows deep symmetrical T-wave inversions across V2-V6, prolonged QTc of 560 ms, and prominent U waves. Troponin is mildly elevated. Echocardiogram shows apical wall motion abnormality. What does this ECG most likely demonstrate?
options: Anterior STEMI requiring emergency coronary angiography
Neurogenic cardiac injury (Neurogenic stunned myocardium) secondary to subarachnoid haemorrhage
Hypokalaemia causing deep T-wave inversions
Type 2 myocardial infarction requiring anticoagulation
Wellens' syndrome indicating critical LAD stenosis
correctAnswerText: Neurogenic cardiac injury (Neurogenic stunned myocardium) secondary to subarachnoid haemorrhage
explanation:
The ECG findings — deep T-wave inversions in V2-V6, prolonged QTc, U waves — in the context of acute SAH represent neurogenic cardiac injury (neurogenic stunned myocardium), not primary ACS.

Option B (Neurogenic cardiac injury secondary to SAH) is CORRECT. SAH causes massive catecholamine release from hypothalamic stimulation, leading to myocardial contraction band necrosis and apical wall motion abnormality (Takotsubo-like pattern). ECG changes including T-wave inversions, QT prolongation, and U waves are well-recognised in SAH. Mild troponin elevation and wall motion abnormalities are neurogenic in origin. The priority remains treating the SAH (neurosurgical assessment, nimodipine, anticonvulsant prophylaxis) — coronary angiography is NOT immediately indicated unless there is haemodynamic instability from arrhythmia.

Option A (Anterior STEMI requiring emergency coronary angiography) is INCORRECT because the clinical context is SAH, not ACS. STEMI shows ST elevation, not T-wave inversions, as the primary finding. Emergency coronary angiography would divert attention from neurosurgical management of the SAH.

Option C (Hypokalaemia) is INCORRECT because hypokalaemia causes U waves and flat T waves but not the deep symmetric T-wave inversions spanning V2-V6 and QTc 560 ms. Potassium would need to be checked but the pattern is neurogenic.

Option D (Type 2 MI) is INCORRECT as the primary diagnosis. While type 2 MI (demand ischaemia) can occur in the context of SAH, the pattern is more consistent with neurogenic stunned myocardium. Anticoagulation must be avoided in acute SAH due to bleeding risk.

Option E (Wellens' syndrome) is INCORRECT because Wellens' syndrome occurs in a pain-free patient after resolved chest pain and indicates critical LAD stenosis. The context here is SAH, not ACS, and the clinical picture completely explains the ECG changes.

label: Hypocalcaemia Ecg
stem: A 38-year-old woman who had a total thyroidectomy 3 days ago presents with perioral tingling, carpopedal spasm, and muscle cramps. Chvostek's and Trousseau's signs are positive. The ECG shown demonstrates prolonged QT interval (QTc 540 ms) predominantly due to a markedly lengthened ST segment (plateau phase). Serum calcium is 1.62 mmol/L (corrected). What does this ECG most likely show?
options: Hyperkalaemia — peaked T waves and wide QRS
Drug-induced QT prolongation from sotalol
QT prolongation consistent with hypocalcaemia — ST segment prolongation due to reduced intracellular calcium
Congenital long QT syndrome type 2
Pericarditis with diffuse ST elevation
correctAnswerText: QT prolongation consistent with hypocalcaemia — ST segment prolongation due to reduced intracellular calcium
explanation:
This post-thyroidectomy patient has hypocalcaemia from inadvertent parathyroid gland removal (hypoparathyroidism). The ECG shows QT prolongation predominantly from ST segment lengthening — the characteristic ECG feature of hypocalcaemia.

Option C (QT prolongation from hypocalcaemia — ST segment prolongation) is CORRECT. Calcium is essential for phase 2 of the cardiac action potential (plateau phase). Low calcium prolongs the plateau, lengthening the ST segment and QTc. Unlike other causes of long QT (drugs, LQTS) where the T wave morphology changes, hypocalcaemia specifically lengthens the ST segment before the T wave. Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm on BP cuff inflation) and Chvostek's sign (facial twitching on percussion) confirm hypocalcaemia clinically. Treatment: IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% over 10 minutes, then oral calcium carbonate and alfacalcidol (active vitamin D) for hypoparathyroidism.

Option A (Hyperkalaemia) is INCORRECT because hyperkalaemia causes peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventual sine-wave pattern — not isolated QT prolongation from ST lengthening with normal T waves.

Option B (Sotalol-induced QT prolongation) is INCORRECT because drug-induced QT prolongation from sotalol or other class III antiarrhythmics changes T-wave morphology (broad or notched T wave) rather than specifically prolonging the ST segment plateau. There is no indication the patient takes sotalol.

Option D (Congenital LQTS type 2) is INCORRECT because congenital LQTS type 2 shows notched, bifid T waves that are triggered by auditory stimuli. The clinical context here is clear post-surgical hypocalcaemia, making a congenital cause irrelevant.

Option E (Pericarditis) is INCORRECT because pericarditis causes saddle-shaped ST elevation with PR depression — not isolated QT prolongation from ST lengthening.

label: Coeliac Disease
stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 1-year history of diarrhoea, bloating, fatigue, and 6 kg weight loss. She has iron deficiency anaemia and elevated anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies. The duodenal biopsy is shown. The image demonstrates flattening of villi (villous atrophy), crypt hyperplasia, and an increased number of intraepithelial lymphocytes. What does this histological section most likely demonstrate?
options: Crohn's disease — granulomatous inflammation with skip lesions
Whipple's disease — PAS-positive macrophages in the lamina propria
Coeliac disease — Marsh type 3 changes with total villous atrophy
Tropical sprue — subtotal villous atrophy in a traveller
Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth — patchy villous blunting
correctAnswerText: Coeliac disease — Marsh type 3 changes with total villous atrophy
explanation:
The duodenal biopsy shows Marsh type 3 changes of coeliac disease: total villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia (compensatory elongation), and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (>25 per 100 enterocytes). Combined with positive anti-tTG IgA antibodies, this confirms coeliac disease.

Option C (Coeliac disease — Marsh type 3 villous atrophy) is CORRECT. Coeliac disease is an immune-mediated enteropathy triggered by dietary gluten (wheat, barley, rye) in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DQ2/DQ8). The Marsh classification grades histological severity. Diagnosis requires consistent serology + compatible histology per NICE NG20. A strict lifelong gluten-free diet is the only treatment. Annual monitoring of TTG antibody levels, FBC, ferritin, B12, folate, calcium, and bone density is recommended.

Option A (Crohn's disease) is INCORRECT because Crohn's affects the small bowel with granulomatous transmural inflammation, skip lesions, and cobblestone mucosa on endoscopy — not villous atrophy. Anti-tTG antibodies would not be positive in Crohn's.

Option B (Whipple's disease) is INCORRECT because Whipple's is caused by Tropheryma whipplei and shows PAS-positive foamy macrophages in the lamina propria, not villous atrophy with intraepithelial lymphocytosis.

Option D (Tropical sprue) is INCORRECT because tropical sprue occurs in individuals from or visiting tropical regions and shows a similar histological picture. However, the UK-based clinical context with positive serology makes coeliac disease the correct diagnosis.

Option E (SIBO) is INCORRECT because SIBO causes patchy mild villous blunting, not the severe total villous atrophy shown here with characteristic crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytosis.

label: Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia
stem: A 48-year-old man is referred with an incidentally elevated WBC of 142 × 10⁹/L on a routine blood test. He is asymptomatic, afebrile, with mild splenomegaly on examination. The blood film shown demonstrates a marked granulocytosis with the complete spectrum of myeloid maturation: myeloblasts, promyelocytes, myelocytes, metamyelocytes, band forms, and mature neutrophils, with eosinophilia and basophilia. What does this blood film most likely demonstrate?
options: Acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) — blast crisis
Chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML) with the Philadelphia chromosome
Leukaemoid reaction from severe infection
Myelofibrosis with leukoerythroblastic picture
Essential thrombocythaemia
correctAnswerText: Chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML) with the Philadelphia chromosome
explanation:
The blood film shows the characteristic complete spectrum of myeloid maturation — from blasts to mature neutrophils — with basophilia and eosinophilia. This pattern with WBC 142 × 10⁹/L and splenomegaly is diagnostic of CML.

Option B (CML with Philadelphia chromosome) is CORRECT. CML is caused by the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene (Philadelphia chromosome — translocation t(9;22)). The blood film shows the hallmark "left shift" with all stages of granulocytic maturation, pronounced basophilia and eosinophilia, and thrombocytosis. Diagnosis is confirmed by detection of BCR-ABL1 by PCR or FISH. First-line treatment is imatinib (or second-generation TKI dasatinib/nilotinib) per NICE guidance, which achieves complete molecular response in most patients.

Option A (AML — blast crisis) is INCORRECT because AML shows >20% blasts with a paucity of maturing cells. The blood film here shows the FULL maturation spectrum with few blasts — the opposite of AML.

Option C (Leukaemoid reaction) is INCORRECT because leukaemoid reactions rarely produce WBC >50 × 10⁹/L and show left shift predominantly of neutrophils/bands without the basophilia and eosinophilia characteristic of CML. A high leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score distinguishes leukaemoid reaction (high LAP) from CML (low LAP).

Option D (Myelofibrosis) is INCORRECT because myelofibrosis causes a leukoerythroblastic picture with teardrop cells (dacrocytes), nucleated red cells, and significant marrow fibrosis. The full granulocytic maturation spectrum without teardrop cells makes myelofibrosis unlikely.

Option E (Essential thrombocythaemia) is INCORRECT because ET primarily causes thrombocytosis with relatively normal WBC and no shift in granulocytic maturation.

label: Pemphigus Vulgaris
stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with widespread painful oral erosions for 3 months, followed by development of flaccid blisters on the scalp, chest, and back. The blisters rupture easily, leaving raw eroded areas. Nikolsky sign is positive. Skin biopsy is shown. The image demonstrates acantholysis (loss of cohesion between keratinocytes) with a suprabasal blister and intact basal layer ("tombstoning"). What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bullous pemphigoid — subepidermal blister with eosinophilia
Dermatitis herpetiformis — subepidermal blister with neutrophil microabscesses
Pemphigus vulgaris — suprabasal acantholysis with anti-desmoglein 3 antibodies
Epidermolysis bullosa — mechanobullous disease from collagen XVII deficiency
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome — subcorneal cleavage
correctAnswerText: Pemphigus vulgaris — suprabasal acantholysis with anti-desmoglein 3 antibodies
explanation:
The histology shows suprabasal acantholysis (loss of keratinocyte-to-keratinocyte adhesion) with the basal layer remaining attached to the basement membrane like a "row of tombstones." This is pathognomonic for pemphigus vulgaris.

Option C (Pemphigus vulgaris — suprabasal acantholysis with anti-desmoglein 3) is CORRECT. Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune blistering disease caused by IgG antibodies against desmoglein 3 (and sometimes desmoglein 1). Desmogleins are desmosomal adhesion proteins — their disruption causes keratinocyte separation (acantholysis). Oral mucosa is involved in nearly all cases (desmoglein 3 predominance). A positive Nikolsky sign (lateral pressure causes blister extension) confirms fragile intraepidermal blistering. Treatment: high-dose oral prednisolone plus rituximab per NICE BAD guidance.

Option A (Bullous pemphigoid) is INCORRECT because BP involves subepidermal blistering (not suprabasal) with IgG antibodies against BP180/BP230 at the basement membrane zone. BP blisters are tense (not flaccid) and oral involvement is less common. Nikolsky sign is negative in BP.

Option B (Dermatitis herpetiformis) is INCORRECT because DH causes intensely pruritic urticarial papules and vesicles on extensor surfaces, with subepidermal blisters containing neutrophil microabscesses (not acantholysis). It is associated with coeliac disease.

Option D (Epidermolysis bullosa) is INCORRECT because EB is a genetic disorder causing mechanobullous disease from trauma. It presents from infancy and does not show acantholysis histologically.

Option E (SSSS) is INCORRECT because staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome occurs predominantly in neonates/young children and shows subcorneal (not suprabasal) cleavage from exfoliative toxins, without acantholysis.

label: Cin Iii
stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to colposcopy after a high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HGSIL) on cervical smear, positive for high-risk HPV (type 16). Colposcopy shows an acetowhite area with punctation at the transformation zone. The biopsy shown demonstrates full-thickness dysplastic squamous cells with loss of normal stratification, nuclear atypia, and frequent mitoses throughout all layers of the epithelium. There is an intact basement membrane with no stromal invasion. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Repeat smear in 3 years — results may represent HPV-related change only
Total hysterectomy as definitive management
Large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) to achieve clearance of CIN 3
Chemotherapy with topical 5-fluorouracil cream
HPV vaccination and repeat colposcopy in 6 months
correctAnswerText: Large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) to achieve clearance of CIN 3
explanation:
The cervical biopsy shows CIN 3 (high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia): full-thickness dysplasia with nuclear atypia, loss of polarity, and frequent mitoses from basal to superficial layers, with intact basement membrane (no invasion). This requires excisional treatment.

Option C (LLETZ — large loop excision of the transformation zone) is CORRECT per NICE NG208. LLETZ is the first-line treatment for CIN 2 and CIN 3 in the UK. It excises the transformation zone under colposcopic guidance using a diathermy loop under local anaesthetic. Cure rates exceed 90% with clear excision margins. Follow-up involves test-of-cure cytology and HPV testing at 6 months post-treatment.

Option A (Repeat smear in 3 years) is INCORRECT because CIN 3 has a significant risk of progression to invasive cervical carcinoma (up to 30% over 10 years if untreated). Watchful waiting is not appropriate for confirmed CIN 3.

Option B (Total hysterectomy) is INCORRECT as first-line management for CIN 3, which is pre-invasive. Hysterectomy may be considered if LLETZ fails, there are positive margins despite re-excision, or there are concurrent gynaecological reasons. It is not the appropriate initial treatment.

Option D (Topical 5-fluorouracil) is INCORRECT because 5-FU has no established role in UK practice for CIN management. It is not recommended in NICE guidelines.

Option E (HPV vaccination) is INCORRECT because vaccination prevents future HPV acquisition but does not treat existing CIN 3. Vaccination is recommended for unvaccinated women up to 25 years old in the UK schedule but does not substitute for excisional treatment.

label: Small Cell Lung Carcinoma
stem: A 67-year-old man with a 50 pack-year smoking history presents with a 3-month history of cough, weight loss, and confusion. He has hyponatraemia (Na⁺ 119 mmol/L) with low serum and high urine osmolality. CT chest shows a central hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. The bronchial biopsy shown demonstrates sheets of small, uniform cells with very little cytoplasm, hyperchromatic nuclei, nuclear moulding, and high mitotic rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung — keratin pearls and intercellular bridges
Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) — oat cell type
Pulmonary carcinoid tumour — neuroendocrine differentiation
Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Mesothelioma — biphasic type
correctAnswerText: Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) — oat cell type
explanation:
The biopsy shows small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC): small cells with very little cytoplasm, dark (hyperchromatic) nuclei, nuclear moulding (cells conform to adjacent cell shape), and a very high mitotic rate. Hyponatraemia from SIADH (ectopic ADH secretion) is a classic paraneoplastic syndrome of SCLC.

Option B (SCLC — oat cell type) is CORRECT. SCLC accounts for 15% of lung cancers, arises from neuroendocrine cells, and is almost exclusively caused by cigarette smoking. The histological features — small oat-shaped cells, nuclear moulding, scant cytoplasm — are pathognomonic. SCLC secretes multiple ectopic hormones causing paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (hyponatraemia — as here), ectopic ACTH (Cushing's), Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, and encephalomyelitis. Treatment: chemotherapy (carboplatin/etoposide) ± immunotherapy (atezolizumab); surgery is rarely possible due to widespread disease at presentation.

Option A (Squamous cell carcinoma) is INCORRECT because SCC shows large polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm, keratin pearl formation, and intercellular bridges. Cells are larger with more cytoplasm than SCLC.

Option C (Carcinoid) is INCORRECT because carcinoid tumours show neuroendocrine differentiation with organoid/trabecular architecture, salt-and-pepper chromatin, and low mitotic rate. They are associated with carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea, wheeze) rather than SIADH.

Option D (Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma) is INCORRECT because LCNEC has large cells (>3× lymphocyte diameter), in contrast to the small cells here with nuclear moulding. Both are aggressive, but histological distinction is essential.

Option E (Mesothelioma) is INCORRECT because mesothelioma arises from the pleura, not the bronchial epithelium, and shows distinctive biphasic epithelioid/sarcomatoid patterns or tubular/papillary formations.

label: Glioblastoma
stem: A 63-year-old man presents with 6 weeks of headache, right-sided weakness, and confusion. MRI shows a ring-enhancing lesion in the left temporal lobe with surrounding oedema and midline shift. Stereotactic biopsy is performed. The histology shown demonstrates hypercellular glial tumour cells with nuclear pleomorphism, microvascular proliferation, and areas of central necrosis surrounded by palisading tumour cells ("pseudopalisading necrosis"). What does this histological section most likely demonstrate?
options: Grade 2 diffuse astrocytoma — IDH-mutant
Primary CNS lymphoma — large B-cell lymphoma
Glioblastoma multiforme (grade 4 astrocytoma) — pseudopalisading necrosis
Brain metastasis from adenocarcinoma
Oligodendroglioma — 1p/19q co-deletion
correctAnswerText: Glioblastoma multiforme (grade 4 astrocytoma) — pseudopalisading necrosis
explanation:
The histology shows glioblastoma multiforme (GBM, WHO grade 4): hypercellular glial tumour with nuclear pleomorphism, microvascular proliferation (glomeruloid vascular tufts), and the pathognomonic pseudopalisading necrosis — tumour cells palisading around central necrotic foci.

Option C (Glioblastoma multiforme — pseudopalisading necrosis) is CORRECT. GBM is the most common and most aggressive primary brain tumour in adults. Pseudopalisading necrosis results from tumour outgrowing its blood supply. Diagnosis requires these histological features. GBM is IDH-wildtype (unlike lower-grade gliomas). MGMT promoter methylation status predicts response to temozolomide chemotherapy. Treatment: surgical resection + radiotherapy + temozolomide (Stupp protocol). Median survival remains approximately 15 months despite treatment.

Option A (Grade 2 astrocytoma) is INCORRECT because low-grade astrocytomas show mild hypercellularity with nuclear atypia but WITHOUT necrosis or microvascular proliferation. These features are by definition absent in grade 2 tumours.

Option B (CNS lymphoma) is INCORRECT because primary CNS lymphoma shows dense perivascular lymphocytic infiltration with large B-cell morphology around blood vessels, not glial cells with pseudopalisading. PCNSL is associated with immunocompromise and responds dramatically to corticosteroids (which can cause diagnostic difficulties).

Option D (Brain metastasis) is INCORRECT because metastases have sharp margins between tumour and adjacent brain and show histological features of the primary tumour (glandular pattern for adenocarcinoma). Metastases are the most common brain tumours overall but show non-glial histology.

Option E (Oligodendroglioma) is INCORRECT because oligodendroglioma shows characteristic "fried egg" cells (clear perinuclear halos) with chicken-wire vascularity, and 1p/19q co-deletion. Pseudopalisading necrosis is absent.

label: Gout
stem: A 55-year-old man with hypertension presents with his third episode of acute painful swelling of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra) over 3 years. He takes bendroflumethiazide. Serum uric acid is 0.58 mmol/L. Joint aspiration is performed. Under polarised light microscopy, the synovial fluid shows needle-shaped crystals that are negatively birefringent (yellow when parallel to the polariser). What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease) — weakly positively birefringent rhomboid crystals
Septic arthritis — gram-positive diplococci in synovial fluid
Reactive arthritis — sterile pyuria in synovial fluid
Gout — negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals
Chondrocalcinosis on X-ray — cartilage calcification
correctAnswerText: Gout — negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals
explanation:
Polarised light microscopy shows negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate (MSU) crystals — the definitive diagnosis of gout. Negative birefringence means the crystals appear yellow when aligned parallel to the slow axis of the compensator.

Option D (Gout — negatively birefringent needle-shaped MSU crystals) is CORRECT. Gout results from MSU crystal deposition in joints, triggered by hyperuricaemia. Bendroflumethiazide causes hyperuricaemia by reducing renal uric acid excretion. The first MTPJ (podagra) is affected in 90% of gout cases. Acute attacks are managed with NSAIDs (naproxen), colchicine, or prednisolone if contraindicated. Prophylaxis with urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol) is started 2–4 weeks after the acute attack settles per NICE CKS, targeting serum uric acid <0.36 mmol/L.

Option A (Pseudogout) is INCORRECT because CPPD crystals are weakly positively birefringent (blue when parallel) and rhomboid-shaped — the opposite of MSU crystals. Pseudogout affects larger joints (knee, wrist) rather than the first MTPJ.

Option B (Septic arthritis) is INCORRECT because septic arthritis would show cloudy fluid with organisms on Gram stain and high WBC. The presence of crystals makes crystal arthropathy the primary diagnosis, though infection should always be considered in an acute monoarthritis.

Option C (Reactive arthritis) is INCORRECT because reactive arthritis follows a triggering infection (urogenital or enteric) and shows sterile fluid with no crystals. It is typically asymmetric, affecting knees, ankles, and sacroiliac joints.

Option E (Chondrocalcinosis) is INCORRECT because chondrocalcinosis is a radiological finding of calcification within cartilage, seen in pseudogout, not the crystal finding described here. It is a separate finding from the polarised microscopy result.

label: Hashimotos Thyroiditis
stem: A 44-year-old woman presents with fatigue, weight gain, constipation, cold intolerance, and dry skin. TSH is 52 mIU/L, free T4 is 6 pmol/L, anti-TPO antibodies are markedly elevated at >600 IU/mL. A thyroid biopsy is performed for an incidental nodule and the histology shown is obtained from the adjacent thyroid tissue. The section demonstrates diffuse lymphocytic infiltration with germinal centre formation, Hürthle cell (oxyphilic) metaplasia of follicular epithelium, and small atrophic thyroid follicles with fibrous tissue replacement. What does this histology most likely demonstrate?
options: De Quervain's thyroiditis — granulomatous inflammation with giant cells
Papillary thyroid carcinoma — psammoma bodies and nuclear grooves
Graves' disease — follicular hyperplasia with scalloped colloid
Hashimoto's thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune lymphocytic thyroiditis)
Simple colloid goitre — normal follicles with stored colloid
correctAnswerText: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune lymphocytic thyroiditis)
explanation:
The thyroid histology shows Hashimoto's thyroiditis: dense lymphocytic infiltration with germinal centre formation, Hürthle cell (oxyphilic/eosinophilic) metaplasia of follicular epithelium, atrophic follicles, and interstitial fibrosis — the classic histological triad.

Option D (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) is CORRECT. Hashimoto's is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions. It is an autoimmune condition characterised by T-lymphocyte-mediated destruction of thyroid follicular cells and anti-TPO/anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. The lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centres (immune reaction) and Hürthle cell change (oxyphilic transformation of damaged epithelium) are pathognomonic. Treatment is levothyroxine replacement titrated to normalise TSH. Monitoring annually for disease progression and complications (including primary thyroid lymphoma — rare) is recommended.

Option A (De Quervain's thyroiditis) is INCORRECT because De Quervain's (subacute granulomatous thyroiditis) shows granulomatous inflammation with giant cells surrounding disrupted follicles and colloid. It follows a viral infection and is self-limiting with transient thyrotoxicosis then hypothyroidism.

Option B (Papillary thyroid carcinoma) is INCORRECT because PTC shows distinctive nuclear features: ground-glass nuclei (Orphan Annie eyes), nuclear grooves, pseudoinclusions, and psammoma bodies. The dominant lymphocyte pattern with germinal centres seen here is autoimmune, not neoplastic.

Option C (Graves' disease) is INCORRECT because Graves' shows follicular hyperplasia with tall columnar epithelium, infolding into lumen, and scalloped/marginated colloid due to TSH receptor stimulation. The histology here shows atrophic follicles, the opposite of Graves.

Option E (Simple colloid goitre) is INCORRECT because colloid goitre shows large follicles distended with colloid and normal flattened epithelium without lymphocytic infiltration or Hürthle cell change.

label: Diabetic Retinopathy
stem: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (diagnosed 10 years ago, poorly controlled, HbA1c 76 mmol/mol) attends diabetic eye screening. The fundus photograph shown demonstrates multiple dot and blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms (tiny red dots), hard exudates (yellow lipid deposits) near the macula, and cotton-wool spots scattered across the retina. There is no new vessel formation. Visual acuity is 6/9 in the right eye. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Central retinal vein occlusion
Hypertensive retinopathy grade 3
Background (non-proliferative) diabetic retinopathy with maculopathy
Proliferative diabetic retinopathy requiring urgent laser photocoagulation
Age-related macular degeneration — drusen formation
correctAnswerText: Background (non-proliferative) diabetic retinopathy with maculopathy
explanation:
The fundus shows background (non-proliferative) diabetic retinopathy with features of maculopathy: dot/blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms, hard exudates near the macula, and cotton-wool spots (nerve fibre layer infarcts). No neovascularisation is visible, confirming pre-proliferative stage with macular involvement.

Option C (Background diabetic retinopathy with maculopathy) is CORRECT. Diabetic maculopathy (hard exudates within one disc diameter of the fovea) is the leading cause of visual loss in type 2 diabetes, affecting central vision. Management includes urgent referral to ophthalmology for consideration of intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy (ranibizumab or bevacizumab) if visual acuity is ≤6/12 and centre-involving oedema is present on OCT, per NICE TA274. Optimising glycaemic control (target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol) and blood pressure (<140/80 mmHg) slows progression.

Option A (CRVO) is INCORRECT because central retinal vein occlusion causes unilateral sudden vision loss with flame haemorrhages in all four quadrants (blood and thunder fundus) and optic disc oedema — not the gradual symmetric changes of diabetic retinopathy.

Option B (Hypertensive retinopathy) is INCORRECT because hypertensive retinopathy shows arteriovenous nipping, flame haemorrhages (follows nerve fibre layer), and papilloedema in severe cases, but not microaneurysms or hard exudates at the macula as the dominant feature.

Option D (Proliferative DR) is INCORRECT because proliferative DR requires the presence of neovascularisation (new vessels on the disc or elsewhere). The fundus here shows no new vessels — this is pre-proliferative/non-proliferative DR.

Option E (AMD) is INCORRECT because AMD shows drusen (pale yellow deposits at the macula), geographic atrophy, or choroidal neovascularisation membranes, not the microaneurysms and haemorrhages of diabetic retinopathy.

label: Papilloedema
stem: A 26-year-old obese woman presents with a 3-month history of daily headaches, worse on lying flat, pulsatile tinnitus, and transient visual obscurations lasting seconds. Visual acuity is preserved at 6/6. The fundoscopy shown demonstrates bilateral optic disc swelling with blurred disc margins, haemorrhages at the disc edge, and loss of physiological cup. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Visual field testing alone and reassurance
MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography, followed by lumbar puncture if imaging is normal
Urgent ophthalmology referral for laser treatment of the optic discs
Temporal artery biopsy to exclude giant cell arteritis
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
correctAnswerText: MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography, followed by lumbar puncture if imaging is normal
explanation:
This obese young woman with headaches, pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations, and bilateral papilloedema has the classic presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, pseudotumour cerebri). Investigation must exclude secondary causes before confirming the diagnosis.

Option B (MRI brain + gadolinium + MR venography, then LP if imaging normal) is CORRECT per NICE NG228. MRI with gadolinium is performed first to exclude secondary causes of raised ICP: space-occupying lesions, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, meningeal carcinomatosis. MR venography assesses for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (particularly relevant in obese women).
