If imaging is normal, lumbar puncture measures opening CSF pressure (>25 cmH₂O in IIH, often 30–50 cmH₂O), confirms diagnosis, and provides therapeutic pressure reduction.

Option A (Visual field testing alone) is INCORRECT because visual field testing is important for monitoring IIH but does not establish the diagnosis or exclude secondary causes. The history warrants urgent full investigation.

Option C (Laser treatment of optic discs) is INCORRECT because the optic disc swelling is from raised ICP — laser treatment of the disc would have no therapeutic effect on the underlying pressure and no role in IIH management.

Option D (Temporal artery biopsy) is INCORRECT because giant cell arteritis is a disease of >50-year-olds with jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and elevated ESR. This 26-year-old's fundoscopic findings represent papilloedema from raised ICP, not ischaemic optic neuropathy from GCA.

Option E (Carotid Doppler) is INCORRECT because pulsatile tinnitus in IIH results from pressure transmitted to the venous sinuses, not carotid stenosis. Carotid Doppler does not investigate raised ICP.

label: Sickle Cell Disease
stem: A 25-year-old man of West African origin presents with severe pain in his lower back and bilateral femur, fever of 38.8°C, and worsening anaemia. He has a known haemoglobinopathy. The blood film shown demonstrates sickle-shaped (crescent/holly-leaf) red cells, target cells, and Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants indicating hyposplenia). Hb is 62 g/L, reticulocytes are elevated at 18%. What is the most appropriate management for this acute vaso-occlusive crisis?
options: Urgent bone marrow biopsy to exclude aplastic crisis
IV iron infusion (ferric carboxymaltose)
IV morphine, aggressive IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, and haematology review — with urgent exchange transfusion if indicated
Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) starting dose 15 mg/kg/day
Platelet transfusion for thrombocytopaenia
correctAnswerText: IV morphine, aggressive IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, and haematology review — with urgent exchange transfusion if indicated
explanation:
This 25-year-old with sickle cell disease (evidenced by the blood film) is having an acute vaso-occlusive crisis (painful sickle crisis): severe bone pain, fever, and worsening anaemia. Immediate pain management and supportive care are the priorities.

Option C (IV morphine, IV fluids, O₂, haematology review, exchange transfusion if indicated) is CORRECT per NICE NG143. Acute sickle crisis management: (1) Analgesia — oral or IV morphine within 30 minutes of arrival (patient controlled analgesia if possible); (2) Oxygen if SpO₂ <95%; (3) IV fluids for hydration; (4) Fever/infection assessment — sickle patients are functionally asplenic (Howell-Jolly bodies confirm hyposplenia) and vulnerable to encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae); (5) Exchange transfusion is indicated in acute chest syndrome, stroke, or severe sequestration.

Option A (Bone marrow biopsy) is INCORRECT as the immediate step. Aplastic crisis (from parvovirus B19) causes a sudden drop in Hb with absent reticulocytes — this patient has elevated reticulocytes (18%), indicating normal bone marrow response.

Option B (IV iron infusion) is INCORRECT because sickle cell patients are not iron deficient — they have a haemolytic anaemia with normal or elevated iron stores. Iron supplementation could worsen iron overload from repeated transfusions.

Option D (Hydroxycarbamide) is INCORRECT as the acute management. Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) is used for prophylaxis of recurrent sickling crises by increasing HbF levels, but is not a treatment for an acute crisis.

Option E (Platelet transfusion) is INCORRECT because there is no thrombocytopaenia mentioned. Platelet transfusion does not address vaso-occlusion.

label: Iron Deficiency Blood Film
stem: A 42-year-old woman with a history of menorrhagia presents with fatigue and breathlessness on climbing stairs. FBC: Hb 81 g/L, MCV 66 fL, MCH 20 pg, RDW elevated. The blood film shown demonstrates microcytic hypochromic red cells, pencil cells (elongated pencil-shaped red cells), anisocytosis (variation in cell size), and a large area of central pallor (>1/3 of red cell diameter). Serum ferritin is 4 mcg/L. What does this blood film most likely demonstrate?
options: Thalassaemia trait — target cells with near-normal iron stores
Anaemia of chronic disease — normocytic normochromic cells
Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic cells with pencil cells
Sideroblastic anaemia — dimorphic red cell population
Vitamin B12 deficiency — macrocytic oval cells with hypersegmented neutrophils
correctAnswerText: Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic cells with pencil cells
explanation:
The blood film shows iron deficiency anaemia (IDA): microcytic hypochromic red cells with central pallor >1/3 of diameter, pencil cells (elliptocytes/poikilocytes), and increased anisocytosis (RDW elevated). Combined with low ferritin (4 mcg/L), MCV 66, and menorrhagia, this is classic IDA.

Option C (Iron deficiency anaemia — microcytic hypochromic with pencil cells) is CORRECT. IDA results from insufficient iron for haem synthesis, causing small, poorly haemoglobinised red cells. Pencil cells are elongated deformed cells from the thin cell membrane of iron-deficient cells. Management: identify and treat the cause (here: investigate and treat menorrhagia), oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg three times daily for at least 3 months (continue for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores) per NICE CKS.

Option A (Thalassaemia trait) is INCORRECT because thalassaemia trait causes microcytosis with near-normal or elevated red cell count, target cells, and NORMAL ferritin. The very low ferritin (4 mcg/L) indicates true iron deficiency, not thalassaemia. Mentzer index (MCV/RBC): <13 favours thalassaemia, >13 favours IDA.

Option B (Anaemia of chronic disease) is INCORRECT because ACD causes normocytic or mildly microcytic anaemia with elevated ferritin (stored iron), reduced serum iron and TIBC. The very low ferritin excludes ACD.

Option D (Sideroblastic anaemia) is INCORRECT because sideroblastic anaemia shows a dimorphic blood film (two populations: normal and microcytic hypochromic) with elevated ferritin and ring sideroblasts on bone marrow iron staining.

Option E (B12 deficiency) is INCORRECT because B12 deficiency causes macrocytic (not microcytic) anaemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes. MCV would be elevated, not reduced.

label: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
stem: A 72-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss over 6 years, worse for high-frequency sounds and in noisy environments. He also reports tinnitus. He denies vertigo, ear pain, or discharge. The pure-tone audiogram shown demonstrates bilateral symmetrical high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with air-bone gap absent (air conduction = bone conduction thresholds), with thresholds of 60 dB at 4000 Hz and 80 dB at 8000 Hz. What does this audiogram most likely demonstrate?
options: Bilateral conductive hearing loss from otosclerosis — elevated bone conduction thresholds
Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss from acoustic neuroma
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss consistent with age-related hearing loss (presbycusis)
Mixed hearing loss from chronic otitis media with effusion
Noise-induced hearing loss at 4 kHz notch — occupational history required
correctAnswerText: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss consistent with age-related hearing loss (presbycusis)
explanation:
The audiogram shows bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) with no air-bone gap — characteristic of presbycusis (age-related hearing loss). The symmetric downward sloping audiogram pattern with loss greatest at high frequencies is pathognomonic.

Option C (Bilateral SNHL consistent with presbycusis) is CORRECT. Presbycusis is the most common cause of hearing loss in older adults, caused by cumulative loss of outer hair cells in the cochlea (predominantly basal turn, responsible for high-frequency hearing). Key features: bilateral, symmetric, high-frequency SNHL, no air-bone gap. Management per NICE NG98: referral to audiology for bilateral digital hearing aids. Hearing aids improve quality of life significantly and reduce risk of social isolation and cognitive decline.

Option A (Conductive hearing loss from otosclerosis) is INCORRECT because otosclerosis causes conductive hearing loss at low frequencies with a characteristic Carhart notch at 2000 Hz and an air-bone gap on audiogram (bone conduction better than air conduction). Otosclerosis typically presents in younger women.

Option B (Unilateral acoustic neuroma) is INCORRECT because acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes asymmetric (typically unilateral) SNHL, often with associated tinnitus and/or vertigo. The bilateral symmetric pattern here is inconsistent with acoustic neuroma.

Option D (Mixed hearing loss from COME) is INCORRECT because chronic otitis media with effusion causes conductive hearing loss (air-bone gap present) with flat or gently sloping audiogram, not the high-frequency SNHL pattern of presbycusis.

Option E (Noise-induced hearing loss) is INCORRECT because noise-induced SNHL characteristically shows a "4000 Hz notch" (isolated dip at 4 kHz) with recovery at higher frequencies. The progressive downsloping loss without a notch in this 72-year-old is more consistent with presbycusis, though an occupational history is relevant.

label: Failure To Thrive
stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred by his GP with faltering growth (previously called failure to thrive). The growth chart shown demonstrates his weight was on the 50th centile at birth and 6 months, but has progressively fallen through centiles — now tracking below the 2nd centile at age 3 years. He has chronic diarrhoea, abdominal distension, and was miserable. He eats poorly. His parents are of normal height. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Bone age X-ray to assess growth potential
Karyotype analysis to exclude Turner syndrome
Full blood count, coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and IgA level
Growth hormone stimulation test
Renal function, urine dipstick and culture
correctAnswerText: Full blood count, coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and IgA level
explanation:
This 3-year-old has organic faltering growth with a crossed centile pattern (downward deviation across centiles), diarrhoea, abdominal distension, and irritability — a classic presentation of coeliac disease.

Option C (FBC, anti-tTG IgA, and serum IgA level) is CORRECT per NICE NG20 and NICE NG75. Coeliac disease is the most common organic cause of faltering growth with GI symptoms in this age group. The anti-tTG IgA is the primary screening test; total IgA is measured simultaneously as IgA deficiency (present in 2–3% of coeliac patients) causes false-negative anti-tTG IgA results. FBC checks for iron deficiency anaemia (a common associated finding in coeliac disease). If anti-tTG IgA >10× upper limit of normal AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive, diagnosis can be made without duodenal biopsy in children.

Option A (Bone age X-ray) is INCORRECT as the first investigation. Bone age is useful for constitutional growth delay or growth hormone deficiency, where there is consistent short stature without GI symptoms or centile crossing. This child has clear organic symptoms warranting coeliac investigation first.

Option B (Karyotype for Turner syndrome) is INCORRECT because Turner syndrome causes short stature in girls, not boys. This patient is male.

Option D (Growth hormone stimulation test) is INCORRECT as first-line because GH deficiency is associated with proportionate short stature, reduced growth velocity, and normal GI findings — not the GI symptoms and centile crossing seen here.

Option E (Renal function and urine culture) is INCORRECT as the first-line investigation, though chronic renal failure can cause faltering growth. The dominant GI features here point to a GI cause, making coeliac disease the primary diagnosis to exclude.

label: Ctg Late Decelerations
stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 39 weeks gestation is in active labour. She has pre-eclampsia with BP 155/98 mmHg on labetalol. The CTG shown demonstrates a baseline fetal heart rate of 155 bpm, reduced variability (<5 bpm for >90 minutes), recurrent late decelerations (decelerations beginning after the peak of each contraction and returning to baseline after the contraction ends), and a rising baseline. The woman is 6 cm dilated. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue monitoring and reassess in 2 hours
Augmentation with IV oxytocin to expedite labour
Immediate category 1 caesarean section (decision-to-delivery <30 minutes)
Fetal blood sampling (FBS) to assess fetal scalp pH
Change maternal position to left lateral, stop oxytocin, and give high-flow oxygen
correctAnswerText: Immediate category 1 caesarean section (decision-to-delivery <30 minutes)
explanation:
This CTG shows pathological features indicating acute fetal compromise: recurrent late decelerations (uteroplacental insufficiency), reduced variability <5 bpm for >90 minutes, and a rising baseline — this is a pathological CTG requiring immediate delivery.

Option C (Immediate category 1 caesarean section — <30 minutes) is CORRECT per NICE CG190 and FIGO CTG interpretation guidelines. Late decelerations with reduced variability and a rising baseline in a pre-eclamptic mother constitute a pathological CTG requiring immediate action. The combination of reduced variability and repetitive late decelerations is a category 1 indication (immediate threat to fetal or maternal life). At 6 cm dilation, caesarean section is the fastest route to delivery. FBS would be contraindicated in pre-eclampsia as the mother cannot safely flex for the procedure under these circumstances.

Option A (Continue monitoring for 2 hours) is INCORRECT because pathological CTG changes (late decelerations with reduced variability) cannot be watched for 2 hours. Fetal acidosis progresses rapidly and permanent neurological injury can result from delay.

Option B (Augment with oxytocin) is INCORRECT because oxytocin increases uterine contraction frequency/amplitude, reducing uteroplacental blood flow further and worsening late decelerations. Oxytocin must be stopped, not started, in this situation.

Option D (Fetal blood sampling) is INCORRECT in this scenario because the CTG shows multiple pathological features (late decels + reduced variability + rising baseline) — immediate delivery is indicated without FBS. FBS is appropriate for equivocal CTG findings.

Option E (Left lateral, stop oxytocin, O₂) is INCORRECT as the definitive response. These are temporising measures in acute decelerations (cord compression) but do not address persistent pathological CTG from uteroplacental insufficiency, which requires immediate delivery.

label: Dress Syndrome
stem: A 38-year-old man with epilepsy developed a widespread morbilliform rash 4 weeks after starting carbamazepine. He now has fever (38.9°C), facial oedema, cervical lymphadenopathy, and hepatomegaly. The blood film shown demonstrates marked peripheral eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes. FBC: WBC 18 × 10⁹/L, eosinophils 3.2 × 10⁹/L. ALT is 380 IU/L. What does this blood film most likely demonstrate, and what is the most important immediate management?
options: Infectious mononucleosis — test for EBV antibodies and manage conservatively
Eosinophilia consistent with DRESS syndrome — immediately stop carbamazepine and start high-dose oral prednisolone
Hypereosinophilic syndrome — start imatinib
Atopic dermatitis exacerbation — topical corticosteroids and antihistamines
Leukaemia — bone marrow biopsy urgently
correctAnswerText: Eosinophilia consistent with DRESS syndrome — immediately stop carbamazepine and start high-dose oral prednisolone
explanation:
The blood film shows eosinophilia with atypical lymphocytes in a patient with fever, rash, facial oedema, lymphadenopathy, and hepatitis starting 4 weeks after carbamazepine — this is the classic presentation of DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms).

Option B (DRESS syndrome — stop carbamazepine + high-dose oral prednisolone) is CORRECT. DRESS is a potentially life-threatening drug hypersensitivity reaction characterised by: skin rash (often morbilliform), fever, lymphadenopathy, facial oedema, internal organ involvement (hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis), and eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes. RegiSCAR score confirms diagnosis. Aromatic antiepileptics (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine) are a common cause. Immediate drug withdrawal is essential; systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg) reduce inflammation and organ damage. The reaction can persist or worsen weeks after drug cessation.

Option A (Infectious mononucleosis) is INCORRECT because EBV causes fever, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes — but without eosinophilia and specifically without the temporal relationship to a new drug. Hepatitis occurs in EBV, but the 4-week drug timeline and eosinophilia point to DRESS.

Option C (Hypereosinophilic syndrome) is INCORRECT because HES requires eosinophils >1.5 × 10⁹/L for >6 months with end-organ damage in the absence of an identifiable cause. Here the cause is clearly carbamazepine-induced DRESS.

Option D (Atopic dermatitis exacerbation) is INCORRECT because atopic dermatitis does not cause systemic features of fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatitis, and eosinophilia. This is not an atopic reaction.

Option E (Leukaemia) is INCORRECT because leukaemia causes anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, and blast cells on blood film. The clinical context — drug exposure, rash, organ involvement — makes DRESS the correct diagnosis.

label: Hearing Loss
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive bilateral hearing loss, worse on the left. She reports no tinnitus, ear discharge, or vertigo. Otoscopy shows intact tympanic membranes bilaterally with a normal light reflex on the right and a slightly dull reflex on the left. The audiogram shown demonstrates the pattern above. What type of hearing loss does this audiogram demonstrate, and what is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Sensorineural hearing loss — most likely presbycusis
Conductive hearing loss — most likely otosclerosis
Mixed hearing loss — most likely chronic suppurative otitis media
Sensorineural hearing loss — most likely noise-induced hearing loss
Conductive hearing loss — most likely acoustic neuroma
correctAnswerText: Conductive hearing loss — most likely otosclerosis
explanation:
The audiogram shows reduced air conduction (AC) thresholds in both ears (hearing loss present) with normal bone conduction (BC) thresholds at 0 dB HL. This air-bone gap — where AC is impaired but BC is preserved — is the hallmark of conductive hearing loss. The pattern is bilateral and worse at all frequencies on the left.

Option B (Conductive hearing loss — otosclerosis) is CORRECT. Otosclerosis is the most common cause of progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss in adults aged 30–50, more common in women. It involves abnormal bone remodelling fixing the stapes footplate in the oval window, preventing transmission of sound vibrations. BC is preserved because the cochlea is unaffected. Classic features: bilateral, progressive, often worse in one ear, no discharge or vertigo, normal otoscopy (or Schwartze sign — flamingo pink blush through tympanic membrane). Treatment: surgical stapedectomy or hearing aids.

Option A (Sensorineural — presbycusis) is INCORRECT. Presbycusis causes sensorineural hearing loss: both AC and BC are reduced equally, with no air-bone gap. It also typically shows high-frequency loss first.

Option C (Mixed hearing loss) is INCORRECT. Mixed hearing loss would show both AC and BC reduced, with AC worse than BC. Here, BC is completely normal.

Option D (Sensorineural — noise-induced) is INCORRECT. Noise-induced hearing loss is sensorineural, characterised by a 4 kHz notch on audiogram with equal AC and BC reduction.

Option E (Acoustic neuroma) is INCORRECT. Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, typically with tinnitus and sometimes vertigo. It does not produce a conductive pattern with air-bone gap.

label: Lbbb
stem: A 67-year-old man with known heart failure presents with sudden-onset chest pain and dyspnoea. His ECG shows broad QRS complexes (>120 ms) with a dominant S wave in V1, broad notched R wave in V5–V6, and discordant ST changes. This is a new ECG finding compared to 6 months ago. What is the significance of this finding?
options: New LBBB in the context of chest pain is equivalent to STEMI and requires urgent reperfusion
LBBB is a normal finding in elderly patients and requires no action
LBBB indicates left ventricular hypertrophy — start antihypertensives
LBBB rules out myocardial infarction as the cause of chest pain
Rate-control medication should be started immediately
correctAnswerText: New LBBB in the context of chest pain is equivalent to STEMI and requires urgent reperfusion
explanation:
The ECG shows left bundle branch block (LBBB) — broad QRS >120 ms, dominant S in V1, notched R in lateral leads, and secondary ST/T changes. Crucially, this is NEW.

Option A is CORRECT. New LBBB in the context of ischaemic symptoms is treated as a STEMI equivalent and triggers the same urgent reperfusion pathway (primary PCI) per NICE NG185 and ESC guidelines. LBBB masks the typical ST changes of MI, making diagnosis of co-existing infarction difficult using standard ECG criteria alone. The Sgarbossa criteria can help identify STEMI in LBBB.

Option B is INCORRECT — new LBBB is never a normal finding to be dismissed.

Option C is INCORRECT — LBBB morphology is distinct from LVH pattern (tall R waves, strain pattern).

Option D is INCORRECT — LBBB does not rule out MI; it makes ECG diagnosis harder.

Option E is INCORRECT — rate control is irrelevant here; the patient is in sinus rhythm with a conduction defect.

label: Svt
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset palpitations and mild dizziness for 45 minutes. She is haemodynamically stable. Her ECG shows a narrow complex tachycardia at 185 bpm with no visible P waves. Vagal manoeuvres (Valsalva) have failed. What is the next most appropriate treatment?
options: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid bolus
IV verapamil 5 mg
Synchronised DC cardioversion at 100 J
IV metoprolol 5 mg
IV digoxin loading dose
correctAnswerText: IV adenosine 6 mg rapid bolus
explanation:
This ECG shows supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) — narrow complex regular tachycardia at 185 bpm with absent P waves (likely concealed in QRS in AVNRT). The patient is haemodynamically stable.

Option A is CORRECT. After failed vagal manoeuvres, IV adenosine 6 mg rapid IV bolus (followed by 12 mg if no response) is first-line pharmacological treatment for stable SVT per Resus UK and NICE. Adenosine blocks the AV node transiently, terminating re-entrant tachycardias. Warn the patient of transient chest tightness and flushing.

Option B is INCORRECT — verapamil is an acceptable alternative but not first-line in the UK and should not be given if adenosine has been used or if broad complex tachycardia is possible.

Option C is INCORRECT — DC cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamically unstable SVT.

Option D is INCORRECT — IV metoprolol can terminate SVT but is slower acting and less effective than adenosine.

Option E is INCORRECT — digoxin has a slow onset and is not used for acute SVT termination.

label: Pancoast Horner
stem: A 59-year-old male smoker presents with right shoulder pain radiating down the medial arm for 3 months. On examination, there is right-sided ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis. His CXR shows an opacity at the right lung apex. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pancoast tumour with Horner's syndrome
Cervical disc herniation at C7–T1
Brachial neuritis (Neuralgic amyotrophy)
Right-sided apical pneumonia
Subclavian artery aneurysm
correctAnswerText: Pancoast tumour with Horner's syndrome
explanation:
The combination of apical lung mass, ipsilateral shoulder/arm pain (T1 nerve root), and Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis — sympathetic chain involvement) is pathognomonic for a Pancoast tumour.

Option A is CORRECT. A Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus tumour) is most commonly non-small cell lung cancer (squamous cell or adenocarcinoma) at the lung apex. It invades adjacent structures: lower brachial plexus (T1 — medial arm pain/weakness), sympathetic chain (Horner's syndrome), subclavian vessels, and ribs. It requires CT/PET staging and MRI for local invasion assessment.

Option B is INCORRECT — cervical disc prolapse at C7–T1 causes radiculopathy but does not cause Horner's syndrome or an apical lung mass.

Option C is INCORRECT — neuralgic amyotrophy causes acute brachial plexus inflammation; it is self-limiting and does not cause an apical mass.

Option D is INCORRECT — apical pneumonia typically presents with acute fever, does not cause Horner's syndrome, and resolves.

Option E is INCORRECT — subclavian aneurysm may cause brachial symptoms but not Horner's + apical mass.

label: Meningococcal Septicaemia
stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents. He has had a fever and vomiting for 6 hours and is now drowsy. On examination, a widespread non-blanching purpuric rash is found across the trunk and legs. The image shown is from his right leg. What is the immediate management?
options: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg immediately, before transfer to PICU
Take blood cultures first and await results before antibiotics
Lumbar puncture to confirm meningococcal disease
IV aciclovir for viral encephalitis
Observe for 4 hours and reassess
correctAnswerText: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg immediately, before transfer to PICU
explanation:
The non-blanching purpuric rash in a febrile, unwell child is meningococcal septicaemia (Neisseria meningitidis) until proven otherwise. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.

Option A is CORRECT. NICE CKS and NICE NG51 mandate immediate IV or IM benzylpenicillin (or IV ceftriaxone if available) WITHOUT waiting for investigations. Antibiotics must not be delayed for blood cultures or LP in a critically ill child. Transfer to PICU/HDU is required urgently.

Option B is INCORRECT — NEVER delay antibiotics waiting for blood cultures in suspected meningococcal disease. Mortality risk is very high without immediate treatment.

Option C is INCORRECT — lumbar puncture is contraindicated in a haemodynamically unstable patient with a coagulopathy/petechial rash until stabilised.

Option D is INCORRECT — aciclovir would be considered for encephalitis (herpes), not septicaemia with purpuric rash.

Option E is INCORRECT — a child with non-blanching purpura and deteriorating consciousness requires immediate treatment, not watchful waiting.

label: Erythema Infectiosum
stem: A 7-year-old child presents with a bright red facial rash on both cheeks giving a "slapped" appearance, followed by a lacy reticular rash on the arms. She has been mildly febrile but is now apyrexial and well. Her mother is 24 weeks pregnant. What is the most important concern?
options: Parvovirus B19 infection — urgent referral of the pregnant mother to obstetrics
Rubella — admit the child for isolation
Scarlet fever — start oral phenoxymethylpenicillin
Roseola infantum — reassure mother
Kawasaki disease — check for coronary artery aneurysm
correctAnswerText: Parvovirus B19 infection — urgent referral of the pregnant mother to obstetrics
explanation:
The clinical picture — bilateral bright red facial rash ("slapped cheeks") followed by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs, in an otherwise well child — is classic of erythema infectiosum (Fifth disease), caused by Parvovirus B19.

Option A is CORRECT. While erythema infectiosum is benign and self-limiting in healthy children, Parvovirus B19 in pregnancy can cause hydrops fetalis (severe foetal anaemia) especially before 20 weeks gestation. A 24-week-pregnant mother exposed to parvovirus B19 requires urgent obstetric referral for maternal serology (IgM/IgG) and foetal surveillance by ultrasound.

Option B is INCORRECT — rubella causes a different rash (pink maculopapular, starting on face, spreading caudally) with postauricular lymphadenopathy.

Option C is INCORRECT — scarlet fever has a sandpaper-textured diffuse rash with circumoral pallor and strawberry tongue, not isolated facial erythema.

Option D is INCORRECT — roseola (HHV-6) causes high fever then rash on the trunk after fever resolves — different pattern.

Option E is INCORRECT — Kawasaki requires 5+ days of fever plus 4 other criteria.

label: Scabies
stem: A 22-year-old university student presents with a 4-week history of intense nocturnal itch affecting his hands, wrists, and genital area. His flatmate has similar symptoms. The image shows linear burrows between the fingers. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Permethrin 5% cream — applied to entire body from neck down, left on for 8–12 hours
Topical hydrocortisone cream for 2 weeks
Oral antihistamine only for symptom relief
Malathion 0.5% lotion — applied only to affected areas
Systemic ivermectin only — topical treatments are ineffective
correctAnswerText: Permethrin 5% cream — applied to entire body from neck down, left on for 8–12 hours
explanation:
Linear burrows (pathognomonic of Sarcoptes scabiei mite tunnelling), nocturnal itch, interdigital and genital distribution, and household contact with similar symptoms confirms scabies.

Option A is CORRECT. Permethrin 5% cream is the recommended first-line treatment in the UK (NICE CKS). It is applied to the entire body surface from the neck downwards (including under fingernails, genitalia, skin folds), left for 8–12 hours, then washed off. A repeat application after 7 days is recommended. All household contacts must be treated simultaneously. Wash all bedding and clothing at 60°C.

Option B is INCORRECT — topical steroids treat itch but not the underlying mite infestation.

Option C is INCORRECT — antihistamines address itch (antihistamine-insensitive in scabies) but do not eradicate the mite.

Option D is INCORRECT — malathion is second-line (first-line in pregnancy as permethrin avoided). It should also be applied to the whole body, not just affected areas.

Option E is INCORRECT — oral ivermectin (unlicensed in UK) is used for crusted (Norwegian) scabies or institutional outbreaks, not routine scabies.

label: Herpes Zoster Dermatomal
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with severe burning pain in a band across the left side of his abdomen followed 3 days later by a vesicular eruption in the same distribution, not crossing the midline. He is otherwise well and immunocompetent. He is seen on day 5 of the rash. Should antiviral therapy be started?
options: No — antiviral treatment is most effective within 72 hours; at day 5 with mild disease it offers minimal benefit. Focus on pain management.
Yes — start IV aciclovir immediately
Yes — start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily regardless of timing
No — antivirals are only for immunocompromised patients
Yes — start oral prednisolone to reduce post-herpetic neuralgia
correctAnswerText: No — antiviral treatment is most effective within 72 hours; at day 5 with mild disease it offers minimal benefit. Focus on pain management.
explanation:
This is herpes zoster (shingles) — unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash following a prodrome of pain. The dermatomal distribution not crossing the midline is pathognomonic.

Option A is CORRECT. Antiviral therapy (aciclovir, valaciclovir, famciclovir) is most effective when started within 72 hours of rash onset. After 72 hours in an otherwise well, immunocompetent patient with mild-moderate disease, the benefit is minimal. Focus should shift to pain management: paracetamol, NSAIDs, and if needed, amitriptyline or gabapentin for neuropathic pain (post-herpetic neuralgia prevention).

Option B is INCORRECT — IV aciclovir is reserved for immunocompromised patients, disseminated disease, ophthalmic or Ramsay Hunt zoster.

Option C is INCORRECT — starting oral antivirals after day 5 in mild uncomplicated zoster in an immunocompetent adult is not recommended.

Option D is INCORRECT — antivirals ARE recommended in immunocompromised patients regardless of timing.

Option E is INCORRECT — oral steroids do not prevent post-herpetic neuralgia and may cause immunosuppression.

label: Rheumatoid Arthritis
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with an 18-month history of symmetrical small joint pain, early morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour, and bilateral hand deformities. The image shows ulnar deviation of the fingers at the MCPJs, swan neck deformities, and subluxation. She is RF and anti-CCP positive. What is the most appropriate initial disease-modifying treatment?
options: Methotrexate (up to 25 mg/week) plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly
Prednisolone 40 mg daily as monotherapy
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy
NSAIDs as sole disease-modifying treatment
Rituximab as first-line biologic
correctAnswerText: Methotrexate (up to 25 mg/week) plus folic acid 5 mg once weekly
explanation:
The classic deformities of established rheumatoid arthritis (RA) — ulnar deviation, swan neck deformity, MCPJ subluxation — with symmetrical polyarthritis, prolonged morning stiffness, positive RF and anti-CCP confirm the diagnosis.

Option A is CORRECT. NICE NG100 recommends methotrexate as the anchor DMARD (disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drug) in RA. It should be started as early as possible. Folic acid 5 mg once weekly (not on the same day as methotrexate) reduces side effects (mucositis, hepatotoxicity). The T2T (treat-to-target) strategy aims for remission or low disease activity.

Option B is INCORRECT — oral steroids are used as short-term bridging therapy during disease flares, not as primary DMARD. Long-term steroid monotherapy causes significant harm.

Option C is INCORRECT — hydroxychloroquine has a mild effect and is used in combination for very mild disease; it is not first-line for established seropositive RA with deformity.

Option D is INCORRECT — NSAIDs reduce inflammation and pain but have no disease-modifying effect.

Option E is INCORRECT — biologics (rituximab, anti-TNF agents) are second-line after failure of ≥2 conventional DMARDs.

label: Gout
stem: A 58-year-old obese man presents with sudden onset severe pain, redness, and swelling of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra) that woke him from sleep. Serum urate is 520 μmol/L. The image shows the affected foot. He is on thiazide diuretics for hypertension. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily
Start allopurinol immediately to lower urate
Oral prednisolone 30–35 mg daily for 5 days
Joint aspiration and intravenous antibiotics
IV colchicine
correctAnswerText: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily
explanation:
Acute podagra — sudden, severe MTP joint pain, erythema, and tenderness in a middle-aged obese man on diuretics — is the classic presentation of gout (urate crystal deposition).

Option A is CORRECT. First-line treatment for acute gout is an NSAID (e.g. naproxen, indomethacin) or colchicine 500 mcg 2–4 times daily. NICE CKS recommends choosing based on comorbidities — NSAIDs are contraindicated in renal impairment/PUD; colchicine is preferred then. A short course (7–10 days) relieves the attack.

Option B is INCORRECT — allopurinol (urate-lowering therapy) must NOT be started during an acute attack as it can prolong or worsen the flare by mobilising urate crystals. Start 2–4 weeks after the acute episode settles.

Option C is INCORRECT — prednisolone is an appropriate second-line option if NSAIDs and colchicine are both contraindicated; not first-line.

Option D is INCORRECT — joint aspiration is appropriate to confirm diagnosis (urate crystals) if uncertain, but antibiotics are only for septic arthritis.

Option E is INCORRECT — IV colchicine is not available in the UK and has a narrow therapeutic index.

label: Peritonsillar Abscess
stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 4 days of severe worsening sore throat, inability to open his mouth fully (trismus), muffled "hot potato" voice, and dysphagia. The image shows unilateral tonsillar swelling with deviation of the uvula to the right, and a bulging of the left soft palate. What is the correct management?
options: Needle aspiration of the abscess plus IV amoxicillin/metronidazole
Oral amoxicillin and discharge
Immediate tonsillectomy under general anaesthetic
Systemic antifungal — this is oral candidiasis
Reassurance — this is severe tonsillitis that will resolve with rest
correctAnswerText: Needle aspiration of the abscess plus IV amoxicillin/metronidazole
explanation:
The unilateral tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation, trismus, and muffled voice with dysphagia is classic peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) — a collection of pus between the tonsillar capsule and superior constrictor muscle.

Option A is CORRECT. Treatment requires both drainage and antibiotics. Needle aspiration (or incision and drainage) is performed under local anaesthetic — this immediately relieves symptoms and confirms pus. IV/oral co-amoxiclav or amoxicillin + metronidazole covers Streptococcus and anaerobes. Hospital admission is appropriate for IV fluids and analgesia if unable to swallow.

Option B is INCORRECT — oral antibiotics alone do not drain the abscess; aspiration/I&D is essential for cure.

Option C is INCORRECT — interval (elective) tonsillectomy is considered after ≥2 quinsy episodes, not as emergency treatment of an acute abscess with trismus.

Option D is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents with white plaques, not tonsillar swelling with uvular deviation.

Option E is INCORRECT — peritonsillar abscess will not resolve without drainage; airway compromise can occur if untreated.

label: Exudative Tonsillitis
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with a 4-day history of severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, fever (38.5°C), and extreme fatigue. Examination reveals bilateral tonsillar enlargement with white exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. The image shows the oropharynx. Monospot test is positive. What is the most important drug to avoid?
options: Amoxicillin — causes a widespread maculopapular rash in EBV infection
Paracetamol — worsens fever in EBV
Ibuprofen — contraindicated in all throat infections
Aciclovir — contraindicated in glandular fever
Prednisolone — never used in EBV infection
correctAnswerText: Amoxicillin — causes a widespread maculopapular rash in EBV infection
explanation:
The clinical picture — bilateral exudative tonsillitis, massive cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, extreme fatigue, and positive monospot — is diagnostic of infectious mononucleosis (glandular fever) caused by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV).

Option A is CORRECT. Amoxicillin (and ampicillin) MUST be avoided in suspected EBV/glandular fever because it causes a widespread maculopapular rash in ~90–100% of cases (the amoxicillin-EBV rash). This is the most important prescribing pitfall. Management is supportive: adequate hydration, analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen), rest, and advice about splenomegaly (avoid contact sports for 4–6 weeks due to splenic rupture risk).

Option B is INCORRECT — paracetamol is appropriate and safe for symptom relief.

Option C is INCORRECT — ibuprofen is appropriate unless there are contraindications (renal disease, asthma). It is not specifically contraindicated in EBV.

Option D is INCORRECT — aciclovir is not effective against EBV and is not contraindicated per se; it just has no benefit.

Option E is INCORRECT — prednisolone is occasionally used for severe tonsillar swelling threatening the airway.

label: Copd Emphysema
stem: A 68-year-old man with a 45 pack-year smoking history presents with a 6-month history of progressive dyspnoea on exertion and a chronic productive cough. His FEV1/FVC ratio is 0.58. The chest X-ray above is obtained. Which of the following radiological findings is most consistent with the diagnosis of COPD/emphysema?
options: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
Unilateral pleural effusion
Bilateral interstitial shadowing in the lower zones
Cardiomegaly with upper lobe blood diversion
correctAnswerText: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
explanation:
The CXR demonstrates the hallmark features of COPD/emphysema: hyperinflated lung fields with increased lucency (air trapping) and flattened hemidiaphragms sitting below the anterior end of the 6th rib.

Option A (Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy) is INCORRECT — this pattern suggests sarcoidosis or lymphoma, not COPD.

Option B (Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms) is CORRECT. Air trapping from loss of elastic recoil pushes the diaphragms flat and increases the AP diameter, giving the classic barrel chest appearance. Diaphragm flattening below the 6th anterior rib is the key diagnostic sign.

Option C (Unilateral pleural effusion) is INCORRECT — not a typical feature of COPD; should prompt investigation for another cause.

Option D (Bilateral basal interstitial shadowing) is INCORRECT — this pattern is seen in pulmonary fibrosis or heart failure, not COPD.

Option E (Cardiomegaly with upper lobe diversion) is INCORRECT — this is the CXR pattern of left ventricular failure.

Clinical pearl: Do not confuse the increased lucency of emphysema with a pneumothorax — in emphysema, lung markings extend symmetrically to the periphery on both sides.

label: Oral Candidiasis
stem: A 58-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 85 cells/µL, not on antiretrovirals) presents with a 2-week history of a sore mouth and difficulty swallowing. Examination reveals white curd-like plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that can be scraped off, leaving a red bleeding base. The clinical photograph above is shown. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL, 1 mL four times daily for 7 days
Fluconazole 150 mg oral single dose
Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
Clotrimazole 1% oral gel four times daily for 14 days
IV amphotericin B
correctAnswerText: Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
explanation:
This clinical photograph shows oral candidiasis (thrush): white curd-like plaques on the tongue that are characteristic of Candida albicans infection. The plaques scrape off to reveal an erythematous, bleeding base — distinguishing thrush from leukoplakia (which cannot be scraped off).

Option A (Nystatin suspension) is INCORRECT as first-line in immunocompromised patients — nystatin is appropriate for mild disease in immunocompetent patients but is less effective in those with significant immunosuppression (CD4 <200).

Option B (Fluconazole 150 mg single dose) is INCORRECT — a single dose is used for vaginal candidiasis; oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients requires a 7–14 day course.

Option C (Fluconazole 50 mg once daily for 7–14 days) is CORRECT. This is the NICE/BNF recommended first-line systemic treatment for oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients. A longer course is used for HIV patients with low CD4 counts.

Option D (Clotrimazole gel) may be used in mild disease in immunocompetent patients but is insufficient here given the degree of immunosuppression and dysphagia suggesting oesophageal involvement.

Option E (IV amphotericin B) is reserved for systemic/invasive candidiasis or fluconazole-resistant disease.

Clinical pearl: Oropharyngeal candidiasis in an adult should prompt investigation for underlying immunosuppression — HIV, haematological malignancy, prolonged corticosteroid use, or diabetes.

label: Onychomycosis
stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 9-month history of a thickened, yellowed, crumbling toenail. The clinical photograph is shown. Nail clippings confirm Trichophyton rubrum on mycology. He takes metformin and simvastatin. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical amorolfine 5% nail lacquer once weekly for 6 months
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
Oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 7 days per month for 3 months (pulse)
No treatment; reassure and advise foot hygiene
correctAnswerText: Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
explanation:
This image shows onychomycosis (tinea unguium): subungual hyperkeratosis with yellow-white discolouration and partial onycholysis, confirmed by positive mycology for Trichophyton rubrum — the most common causative organism.

Option A (Topical amorolfine alone) is INCORRECT for established toenail infection with matrix involvement — topical agents cannot penetrate the nail plate sufficiently and are only suitable for mild distal disease without matrix involvement.

Option B (Terbinafine 6 weeks) is the correct duration for fingernail onychomycosis, not toenails.

Option C (Terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12–16 weeks) is CORRECT. This is the NICE/BNF first-line treatment for dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis. Check LFTs before starting — terbinafine is hepatotoxic. A key PLAB distinction: fingernails need 6 weeks, toenails need 12–16 weeks.

Option D (Itraconazole pulse) is an alternative for patients intolerant of terbinafine, or for non-dermatophyte/Candida infections.

Option E (No treatment) may be appropriate for asymptomatic elderly patients without comorbidities, but is incorrect here — diabetes increases the risk of secondary bacterial infection, cellulitis, and spread.

Clinical pearl: Always confirm with mycology before prescribing systemic antifungals. Psoriatic nail disease can closely mimic onychomycosis.

label: Atrial Flutter
stem: A 61-year-old man with hypertension and mild mitral regurgitation presents with a 4-hour history of palpitations and mild breathlessness. BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 150 bpm regular, SpO₂ 97%. The ECG above is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate initial management?
options: Atrial fibrillation — metoprolol 5 mg IV
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — immediate DC cardioversion
SVT — adenosine 6 mg IV bolus
Ventricular tachycardia — amiodarone 300 mg IV
correctAnswerText: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
explanation:
This ECG shows classic atrial flutter: regular sawtooth flutter waves (F waves) at ~300 bpm, best seen in leads II, III, aVF and V1, with a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm due to 2:1 AV conduction. A regular tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm should always raise suspicion for atrial flutter with 2:1 block.

Option A (AF with metoprolol IV) is INCORRECT — AF produces an irregularly irregular rhythm with no organised atrial activity, not the regular sawtooth pattern seen here.

Option B (Atrial flutter 2:1 — bisoprolol orally) is CORRECT for this haemodynamically stable patient. Rate control with a beta-blocker (or rate-limiting calcium channel blocker such as diltiazem or verapamil) is first-line per NICE NG196 for stable atrial flutter.

Option C (DC cardioversion) is indicated if haemodynamically unstable (hypotension, pulmonary oedema, syncope) or for elective rhythm control after anticoagulation assessment — not first-line in a stable patient.

Option D (Adenosine for SVT) is INCORRECT as definitive treatment. Adenosine increases AV block and can transiently unmask flutter waves (useful diagnostically) but does not terminate flutter.

Option E (Amiodarone for VT) is INCORRECT — VT produces a broad complex tachycardia; this ECG shows narrow complexes.

Clinical pearl: HR of exactly 150 bpm = atrial flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise. Consider anticoagulation assessment in the same way as AF.

label: Lvh
stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension attends for routine review. BP today is 168/102 mmHg. He is asymptomatic. The ECG above is obtained. Which of the following best describes the finding and its clinical significance?
options: Right ventricular hypertrophy — suggests cor pulmonale; arrange CTPA
Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
Left bundle branch block — urgent cardiology referral for pacing assessment
Anterior STEMI — call the cath lab immediately
Normal ECG variant — no action required
correctAnswerText: Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
explanation:
This ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH): tall R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V4–V6), deep S waves in right precordial leads (V1–V2), and a lateral strain pattern (ST depression with T-wave inversion in V5–V6, I, aVL).

Diagnostic voltage criteria:
• Sokolow–Lyon: S(V1) + R(V5 or V6) ≥35 mm
• Cornell: R(aVL) + S(V3) ≥28 mm in men

Option A (RVH) is INCORRECT — RVH shows tall R in V1, right axis deviation, and deep S in lateral leads — the opposite pattern.

Option B (LVH — intensify treatment) is CORRECT. LVH is a NICE-defined marker of hypertensive end-organ damage, significantly increasing cardiovascular risk. It mandates intensification of antihypertensives targeting BP <130/80 mmHg and consideration of echocardiography.

Option C (LBBB) is INCORRECT — LBBB requires broad QRS >120 ms with M-pattern in V5/V6; QRS here is narrow.

Option D (Anterior STEMI) is INCORRECT — there is no ST elevation; the lateral ST depression is the LV strain pattern secondary to hypertrophy.

Option E (Normal variant) is INCORRECT — voltage criteria are clearly exceeded.

Clinical pearl: LVH strain pattern (lateral ST depression/T-inversion) is frequently confused with lateral ischaemia — context is key: longstanding hypertension + voltage criteria = LVH strain.

label: Geographic Tongue
stem: A 28-year-old woman attends her GP concerned about the appearance of her tongue, which she noticed 2 weeks ago. She reports mild sensitivity to spicy and acidic foods but no pain at rest, no fever, and no difficulty swallowing. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history. Examination reveals the finding shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prescribe oral fluconazole for 7 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
Take a tongue biopsy to exclude dysplasia
Prescribe topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellets)
correctAnswerText: Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
explanation:
This image shows geographic tongue (benign migratory glossitis): irregularly shaped erythematous patches with white serpiginous borders on the dorsal tongue, representing areas of desquamated filiform papillae. The map-like pattern migrates over days to weeks.

Option C is CORRECT. Geographic tongue is a benign, self-limiting inflammatory condition. No treatment is required. Management is reassurance plus avoidance of triggers (spicy/acidic food, alcohol, tobacco).

Option A (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw red base. Geographic tongue has erythematous patches with fixed white borders and no removable coating.

Option B (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — geographic tongue is benign. Red flags for oral cancer include indurated non-healing ulcers and persistent white/red patches (leukoplakia/erythroplakia) for >3 weeks. None are present here.

Option D (Biopsy) is INCORRECT — biopsy is not indicated for classic geographic tongue without suspicious features.

Option E (Topical corticosteroids) may reduce symptoms in severe symptomatic cases but is not first-line for a mildly symptomatic presentation.

Clinical pearl: Geographic tongue is associated with psoriasis and atopy. The key distinction from candidiasis — candidal plaques are white and removable; geographic tongue patches are smooth red areas that cannot be wiped off.

label: Cyanosis
stem: A 68-year-old man with a long history of COPD presents to the emergency department with worsening breathlessness over 3 days. He smokes 20 cigarettes/day. Examination of the hands reveals the finding shown. Oxygen saturation is 84% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min
Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
Salbutamol nebuliser only and reassess in 1 hour
Oral prednisolone 40 mg and discharge with community follow-up
Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude pulmonary embolism
correctAnswerText: Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
explanation:
The image shows peripheral cyanosis: blue-purple discolouration of the nail beds and fingertips due to increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in peripheral capillaries.

Option B is CORRECT. In known COPD (a CO₂-retaining condition), controlled oxygen therapy targeting SpO₂ 88–92% is mandated. A subset of COPD patients have chronic hypercapnia and depend on their hypoxic drive for ventilation. Uncontrolled high-flow oxygen suppresses this drive, worsens hypercapnia, and precipitates type 2 respiratory failure. A Venturi mask at 24–28% FiO₂ is the delivery device of choice, with ABG to monitor CO₂.

Option A (15 L/min high-flow O₂) is INCORRECT and potentially lethal in COPD — this is the standard approach for non-COPD patients (e.g. pneumonia, trauma) where target SpO₂ is 94–98%.

Option C (Salbutamol only) is insufficient for SpO₂ 84%.

Option D (Oral prednisolone + discharge) is dangerous — this patient requires hospital admission.

Option E (CTPA) is not the immediate priority; correct the acute hypoxaemia first.

Clinical pearl: The single highest-yield oxygen target distinction in PLAB — COPD = 88–92%; everyone else = 94–98%. Always check the diagnosis before reaching for the oxygen mask.

label: Herpes Labialis
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-day history of painful, tingling blisters on her upper lip. She reports a similar episode 6 months ago that resolved spontaneously. She is otherwise well with no fever. The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily
Reassure — no treatment required; will resolve in 48 hours
correctAnswerText: Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
explanation:
The image shows herpes labialis (cold sore): a cluster of painful vesicles on the vermilion border of the lip, caused by reactivation of latent HSV-1.

Option B is CORRECT. NICE CKS recommends topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days as first-line treatment in an immunocompetent patient. Efficacy is greatest when started at the first sign of tingling (prodrome).

Option A (Oral aciclovir) is reserved for severe or widespread HSV, immunocompromised patients, eczema herpeticum, or herpetic keratoconjunctivitis — not a straightforward cold sore.

Option C (Fluconazole) is an antifungal — ineffective against HSV.

Option D (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED in viral infections — steroids suppress local immunity and may worsen HSV spread.

Option E (Reassure only) is INCORRECT — untreated cold sores take 7–10 days to resolve; topical aciclovir shortens duration and reduces symptoms.

Clinical pearl: The prodromal tingling before blisters appear is the optimal treatment window. Counsel patients to keep topical aciclovir at home and start it at first tingle.

label: Aphthous Ulcer
stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to her GP with a painful lesion on the inside of her lower lip for 4 days, shown above. She reports this is her third episode in 6 months. She has no systemic symptoms, no weight loss, and no diarrhoea. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Full blood count, B12, folate, ferritin, and anti-TTG antibodies
Topical aciclovir cream applied to the ulcer
correctAnswerText: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
explanation:
The image shows a minor aphthous ulcer: a well-defined, round/oval shallow ulcer with a white/grey pseudomembranous base and an erythematous halo on the labial mucosa.

Option A is CORRECT. First-line management per NICE CKS:
• Topical chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash — reduces secondary infection and pain
• Topical corticosteroid — hydrocortisone oromucosal 2.5 mg pellet four times daily (or beclometasone inhaler as mouthwash) — shortens healing time

Option B (Oral aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aphthous ulcers are NOT caused by HSV. Herpetic ulcers are smaller, more numerous, preceded by vesicles, and occur on keratinised mucosa (hard palate, gingiva); aphthous ulcers occur on non-keratinised mucosa (labial, buccal, floor of mouth).

Option C (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — red flags for oral cancer requiring urgent referral are: unexplained ulcer persisting >3 weeks, induration, associated lymphadenopathy, or risk factors (smoking, alcohol). None apply here.

Option D (Bloods) is appropriate for recurrent aphthous ulcers to exclude underlying causes (iron, B12/folate deficiency, coeliac disease), but treatment should be initiated concurrently.

Option E (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aciclovir is antiviral; aphthous ulcers have no viral aetiology.

Clinical pearl: Location is the key distinction — aphthous on non-keratinised (mobile) mucosa; herpetic on keratinised (fixed) mucosa such as the hard palate and attached gingiva.

label: Tinea Pedis
stem: A 24-year-old male university student presents with a 3-week history of itchy, peeling, and macerated skin between his toes and on the sole of his foot, as shown. He uses a communal shower at his gym. There is no nail involvement. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks
Oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 4 weeks
Topical fusidic acid cream twice daily for 7 days
correctAnswerText: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
explanation:
The image shows tinea pedis (athlete's foot): scaling, maceration, and peeling of the interdigital web spaces and plantar surface, caused by dermatophyte fungi (most commonly Trichophyton rubrum).

Option A is CORRECT. Topical antifungal is first-line for tinea pedis without nail involvement. NICE CKS recommends clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks (or terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1 week — shorter, equally effective).

Option B (Oral terbinafine) is second-line — reserved for topical treatment failure, extensive/moccasin-type tinea pedis, or onychomycosis (nail involvement). Not appropriate as initial therapy here.

Option C (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED as monotherapy in fungal infections. Steroids suppress local immunity, worsen tinea, and can cause tinea incognito — an atypical altered appearance that delays diagnosis.

Option D (Oral fluconazole) is second-line and not indicated as initial therapy for uncomplicated tinea pedis.

Option E (Topical fusidic acid) is an antibacterial — ineffective against dermatophyte fungi.

Clinical pearl: Always consider fungal infection before prescribing topical steroids for an itchy rash on the foot. Counsel on hygiene: dry thoroughly between toes, wear cotton socks, change footwear regularly, avoid communal barefoot areas.

label: Oral Leukoplakia
stem: A 58-year-old man who smokes 20 cigarettes per day and drinks 30 units of alcohol per week is referred by his dentist. He has a painless white patch on the lateral border of his tongue that cannot be wiped off, present for 6 weeks, as shown. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
Prescribe oral fluconazole 50 mg daily for 7 days and review in 2 weeks
Apply topical aciclovir and review in 1 week
Reassure and advise smoking cessation; review in 3 months
Prescribe topical nystatin suspension and review in 2 weeks
correctAnswerText: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
explanation:
The image shows oral leukoplakia: a white patch on the lateral border of the tongue that cannot be wiped off. This is a potentially malignant disorder with a 1–5% risk of transformation to oral squamous cell carcinoma. Risk factors here: age >50, heavy smoking, heavy alcohol use — all present.

Option A is CORRECT. NICE NG12 mandates an urgent 2-week-wait (2WW) referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for any unexplained white or red patch in the mouth lasting >3 weeks, particularly in patients with tobacco use, alcohol excess, or age >45. Biopsy is required to exclude dysplasia or carcinoma-in-situ.

Option B (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw erythematous base. This lesion does NOT wipe off — the critical distinguishing feature.

Option C (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — leukoplakia is not viral in aetiology.

Option D (Reassure + smoking cessation) is INCORRECT. Smoking cessation is essential long-term but does not substitute for urgent biopsy when a red-flag lesion is present. A 3-month delay is dangerous.

Option E (Nystatin) is for oral candidiasis — incorrect diagnosis.

Clinical pearl: Do NOT be reassured by the absence of pain — early oral cancer and leukoplakia with dysplasia are typically painless. The triad to memorise: non-wipeable white/red patch + >3 weeks + tobacco/alcohol = 2WW referral.

label: COPD / Emphysema
vignette: A 68-year-old man with a 45 pack-year smoking history presents with a 6-month history of progressive dyspnoea on exertion and a chronic productive cough. His FEV1/FVC ratio is 0.58. The chest X-ray above is obtained.
stem: Which of the following radiological findings is most consistent with the diagnosis of COPD/emphysema on this chest X-ray?
options: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
Unilateral pleural effusion
Bilateral interstitial shadowing in the lower zones
Cardiomegaly with upper lobe blood diversion
correctAnswerText: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened hemidiaphragms
explanation: This CXR demonstrates the hallmark features of COPD/emphysema: hyperinflated lung fields with increased lucency (air trapping), flattened hemidiaphragms (seen below the anterior end of the 6th rib), and a barrel-shaped chest. The increased AP diameter reflects chronic air trapping. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy would suggest sarcoidosis or lymphoma. A unilateral pleural effusion is not a feature of COPD. Bilateral basal interstitial shadowing is more consistent with pulmonary fibrosis. Cardiomegaly with upper lobe diversion suggests left heart failure.

label: Oral Candidiasis (Thrush)
vignette: A 58-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 85 cells/µL, not on antiretrovirals) presents with a 2-week history of a sore mouth and difficulty swallowing.
stem: Examination reveals white curd-like plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that can be scraped off, leaving a red bleeding base. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/mL four times daily for 7 days
Fluconazole 150 mg oral single dose
Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
Clotrimazole 1% oral gel four times daily for 14 days
IV amphotericin B
correctAnswerText: Fluconazole 50 mg orally once daily for 7–14 days
explanation:
This image shows oral candidiasis (thrush): white curd-like plaques that scrape off to reveal a bleeding erythematous base, distinguishing thrush from leukoplakia.

Option C (Fluconazole 50 mg once daily for 7–14 days) is CORRECT — NICE/BNF first-line systemic treatment for oropharyngeal candidiasis in immunocompromised patients.

Option A (Nystatin) is first-line only in mild disease in immunocompetent patients; less effective in significant immunosuppression.

Option B (Fluconazole 150 mg single dose) is used for vaginal candidiasis, not oropharyngeal.

Option D (Clotrimazole gel) is insufficient in immunosuppressed patients, especially with dysphagia suggesting oesophageal involvement.

Option E (IV amphotericin B) is reserved for systemic/invasive or fluconazole-resistant candidiasis.

label: Onychomycosis (Fungal Nail Infection)
vignette: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with a 9-month history of a thickened, yellowed, crumbling toenail. Nail clippings confirm Trichophyton rubrum on mycology. He takes metformin and simvastatin.
stem: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Topical amorolfine 5% nail lacquer once weekly for 6 months
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
Oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 7 days per month for 3 months (pulse)
No treatment; reassure and advise foot hygiene
correctAnswerText: Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 12–16 weeks
explanation:
This image shows onychomycosis (tinea unguium): subungual hyperkeratosis with yellow-white discolouration and partial onycholysis, confirmed by Trichophyton rubrum mycology.

Option C (Terbinafine 250 mg daily for 12–16 weeks) is CORRECT — NICE/BNF first-line for dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis. Key PLAB distinction: fingernails = 6 weeks, toenails = 12–16 weeks. Check LFTs before starting.

Option A (Topical amorolfine alone) is insufficient for established toenail infection with matrix involvement — topicals cannot penetrate the nail plate adequately.

Option B (6 weeks) is the correct duration for fingernails only.

Option D (Itraconazole pulse) is an alternative for terbinafine-intolerant patients or non-dermatophyte/Candida infections.

Option E (No treatment) would be appropriate in asymptomatic elderly patients without comorbidities, but not here — diabetes increases risk of secondary bacterial infection and cellulitis.

Clinical pearl: Always confirm with mycology before prescribing systemic antifungals. Psoriatic nail disease closely mimics onychomycosis.

label: Atrial Flutter
vignette: A 61-year-old man with hypertension and mild mitral regurgitation presents with a 4-hour history of palpitations and mild breathlessness. BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 150 bpm regular, SpO₂ 97%.
stem: The ECG above is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate initial management?
options: Atrial fibrillation — metoprolol 5 mg IV
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — immediate DC cardioversion
SVT — adenosine 6 mg IV bolus
Ventricular tachycardia — amiodarone 300 mg IV
correctAnswerText: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — bisoprolol 5 mg orally for rate control
explanation:
This ECG shows classic atrial flutter: regular sawtooth F waves at ~300 bpm best seen in II, III, aVF and V1, with a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm due to 2:1 AV conduction.

Option B (Atrial flutter 2:1 — bisoprolol orally) is CORRECT for a haemodynamically stable patient. Rate control with a beta-blocker or rate-limiting calcium channel blocker is first-line per NICE NG196.

Option A (AF) is incorrect — AF is irregularly irregular with no organised atrial activity.

Option C (DC cardioversion) is for haemodynamic instability or elective rhythm control, not first-line in a stable patient.

Option D (Adenosine) can unmask flutter waves diagnostically but does not terminate flutter.

Option E (Amiodarone for VT) is incorrect — VT is broad complex; this is narrow complex.

Clinical pearl: HR of exactly 150 bpm = atrial flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise.

label: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)
vignette: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension attends for routine review. BP today is 168/102 mmHg. He is asymptomatic.
stem: The ECG above is obtained. Which of the following best describes the finding and its clinical significance?
options: Right ventricular hypertrophy — suggests cor pulmonale; arrange CTPA
Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
Left bundle branch block — urgent cardiology referral for pacing assessment
Anterior STEMI — call the cath lab immediately
Normal ECG variant — no action required
correctAnswerText: Left ventricular hypertrophy — confirms hypertensive end-organ damage; intensify antihypertensive therapy
explanation:
This ECG shows LVH: tall R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V4–V6), deep S waves in V1–V2, and lateral strain pattern (ST depression with T-wave inversion in V5–V6).

Voltage criteria: Sokolow–Lyon: S(V1) + R(V5 or V6) ≥35 mm; Cornell: R(aVL) + S(V3) ≥28 mm (men).

Option B is CORRECT — LVH is a NICE-defined marker of hypertensive end-organ damage, mandating intensification of antihypertensives to BP <130/80 mmHg and echocardiography.

Option A (RVH) shows the opposite pattern: tall R in V1, right axis deviation.

Option C (LBBB) requires broad QRS >120 ms; QRS here is narrow.

Option D (Anterior STEMI) — no ST elevation; the lateral ST depression is LV strain, not ischaemia.

Option E (Normal) — voltage criteria clearly exceeded.

Clinical pearl: LVH strain pattern mimics lateral ischaemia — distinguish by context (longstanding hypertension + voltage criteria) rather than ST changes alone.

label: Geographic Tongue (Benign Migratory Glossitis)
vignette: A 28-year-old woman attends her GP concerned about the appearance of her tongue, which she noticed 2 weeks ago. She reports mild sensitivity to spicy and acidic foods but no pain at rest, no fever, and no difficulty swallowing. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history.
stem: Examination reveals the finding shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prescribe oral fluconazole for 7 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
Take a tongue biopsy to exclude dysplasia
Prescribe topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellets)
correctAnswerText: Reassure and advise avoidance of known triggers
explanation:
This image shows geographic tongue (benign migratory glossitis): irregularly shaped erythematous patches with white serpiginous borders on the dorsal tongue, representing desquamation of filiform papillae. The map-like pattern migrates over days to weeks.

Option C is CORRECT. Geographic tongue is benign and self-limiting. Management is reassurance plus avoidance of triggers (spicy/acidic food, alcohol, tobacco).

Option A (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis presents as white pseudomembranous plaques that wipe off, leaving a raw red base; geographic tongue patches do not wipe off.

Option B (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — oral cancer red flags include indurated non-healing ulcers and persistent leukoplakia/erythroplakia >3 weeks; none present here.

Option D (Biopsy) is INCORRECT — not indicated for classic geographic tongue without suspicious features.

Option E (Topical corticosteroids) may help in severe symptomatic cases but is not first-line here.

Clinical pearl: Associated with psoriasis and atopy. Key distinction from candidiasis: candidal plaques are white and wipe off; geographic tongue has smooth red patches with white borders that cannot be removed.

label: Peripheral Cyanosis
vignette: A 68-year-old man with a long history of COPD presents to the emergency department with worsening breathlessness over 3 days. He smokes 20 cigarettes/day. Examination of the hands reveals the finding shown. Oxygen saturation is 84% on air.
stem: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min
Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
Salbutamol nebuliser only and reassess in 1 hour
Oral prednisolone 40 mg and discharge with community follow-up
Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude pulmonary embolism
correctAnswerText: Controlled oxygen therapy titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%, with arterial blood gas monitoring
explanation:
The image shows peripheral cyanosis: blue-purple discolouration of the nail beds and fingertips due to increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in peripheral capillaries.

Option B is CORRECT. In known COPD, controlled oxygen targeting SpO₂ 88–92% is mandated. Chronic hypercapnic COPD patients depend on hypoxic drive for ventilation — uncontrolled high-flow oxygen suppresses this drive, worsens CO₂ retention, and precipitates type 2 respiratory failure. Use a Venturi mask at 24–28% FiO₂ with ABG monitoring.

Option A (15 L/min high-flow O₂) is INCORRECT in COPD — the standard 94–98% target does NOT apply. This is potentially fatal.

Option C (Salbutamol only) is insufficient for SpO₂ 84%.

Option D (Discharge on oral prednisolone) is dangerous — this patient needs hospital admission.

Option E (CTPA) is not the immediate priority; correct hypoxaemia first.

Clinical pearl: COPD = target SpO₂ 88–92%. Everyone else = 94–98%. This is the highest-yield oxygen distinction in PLAB.

label: Herpes Labialis (Cold Sore)
vignette: A 22-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-day history of painful, tingling blisters on her upper lip. She reports a similar episode 6 months ago that resolved spontaneously. She is otherwise well with no fever.
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily
Reassure — no treatment required; will resolve in 48 hours
correctAnswerText: Topical aciclovir 5% cream applied five times daily for 5 days
explanation:
The image shows herpes labialis (cold sore): painful vesicles on the vermilion border of the lip due to reactivation of latent HSV-1.

Option B is CORRECT. NICE CKS recommends topical aciclovir 5% cream five times daily for 5 days as first-line treatment in immunocompetent patients. Most effective when started at the prodromal tingling stage.

Option A (Oral aciclovir) is reserved for severe/widespread HSV, immunocompromised patients, eczema herpeticum, or herpetic keratoconjunctivitis — not a straightforward cold sore.

Option C (Fluconazole) is an antifungal — ineffective against HSV.

Option D (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED — steroids suppress local immunity and can worsen HSV spread.

Option E (Reassure only) is INCORRECT — cold sores take 7–10 days untreated; topical aciclovir shortens this.

Clinical pearl: Start topical aciclovir at first tingle — before blisters appear — for maximum benefit.

label: Aphthous Ulcer (Mouth Ulcer)
vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents to her GP with a painful lesion on the inside of her lower lip for 4 days. She reports this is her third episode in 6 months. She has no systemic symptoms, no weight loss, and no diarrhoea. She is otherwise well.
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
Oral aciclovir 200 mg five times daily for 5 days
Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral surgery
Full blood count, B12, folate, ferritin, and anti-TTG antibodies
Topical aciclovir cream applied to the ulcer
correctAnswerText: Topical chlorhexidine mouthwash and topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone oromucosal pellet)
explanation:
The image shows a minor aphthous ulcer: a well-defined round/oval shallow ulcer with a white/grey pseudomembranous base and an erythematous halo on the labial mucosa.

Option A is CORRECT. NICE CKS first-line: topical chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash (reduces secondary infection and pain) plus topical corticosteroid — hydrocortisone oromucosal 2.5 mg pellet four times daily — to shorten healing time.

Option B (Oral aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aphthous ulcers are NOT caused by HSV. Herpetic ulcers are smaller, more numerous, vesicle-preceded, and occur on keratinised mucosa (hard palate, gingiva). Aphthous ulcers occur on non-keratinised mucosa (labial, buccal, floor of mouth).

Option C (2WW referral) is INCORRECT — no red flags here. Urgent referral for: unexplained ulcer >3 weeks, induration, lymphadenopathy, or high-risk history (smoking, alcohol).

Option D (Bloods) is appropriate for recurrent aphthous ulcers to exclude iron/B12/folate deficiency or coeliac disease, but treatment should also be started.

Option E (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — aciclovir is antiviral; aphthous ulcers have no viral cause.

Clinical pearl: Location distinguishes aphthous from herpetic — aphthous on non-keratinised (mobile) mucosa; herpetic on keratinised (fixed) mucosa.

label: Tinea Pedis (Athlete's Foot)
vignette: A 24-year-old male university student presents with a 3-week history of itchy, peeling, and macerated skin between his toes and on the sole of his foot. He uses a communal shower at his gym. There is no nail involvement.
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
Oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily for 6 weeks
Topical hydrocortisone 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks
Oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 4 weeks
Topical fusidic acid cream twice daily for 7 days
correctAnswerText: Topical clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks
explanation:
The image shows tinea pedis (athlete's foot): scaling, maceration, and peeling of the interdigital web spaces and plantar surface, caused by dermatophyte fungi (most commonly Trichophyton rubrum).

Option A is CORRECT. Topical antifungal is first-line for tinea pedis without nail involvement. NICE CKS recommends clotrimazole 1% cream twice daily for 4 weeks, or terbinafine 1% cream once daily for 1 week (shorter, equally effective).

Option B (Oral terbinafine) is second-line — for topical treatment failure, extensive/moccasin-type disease, or onychomycosis. Not first-line here.

Option C (Topical hydrocortisone) is CONTRAINDICATED as monotherapy — steroids worsen tinea and can cause tinea incognito.

Option D (Oral fluconazole) is second-line; not initial therapy for uncomplicated tinea pedis.

Option E (Topical fusidic acid) is antibacterial — ineffective against dermatophytes.

Clinical pearl: Consider fungal infection before prescribing topical steroids for any itchy foot rash. Hygiene advice: dry between toes, cotton socks, change footwear regularly.

label: Oral Leukoplakia
vignette: A 58-year-old man who smokes 20 cigarettes per day and drinks 30 units of alcohol per week is referred by his dentist. He has a painless white patch on the lateral border of his tongue that cannot be wiped off, present for 6 weeks.
stem: The appearance is shown. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
Prescribe oral fluconazole 50 mg daily for 7 days and review in 2 weeks
Apply topical aciclovir and review in 1 week
Reassure and advise smoking cessation; review in 3 months
Prescribe topical nystatin suspension and review in 2 weeks
correctAnswerText: Urgent 2-week-wait referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for biopsy
explanation:
The image shows oral leukoplakia: a non-wipeable white patch on the lateral tongue — a potentially malignant disorder with 1–5% risk of SCC transformation. Risk factors: age >50, heavy smoking, heavy alcohol — all present.

Option A is CORRECT. NICE NG12 mandates urgent 2WW referral to oral and maxillofacial surgery for any unexplained white/red oral patch lasting >3 weeks, especially with tobacco, alcohol, or age >45. Biopsy is required to exclude dysplasia or carcinoma-in-situ.

Option B (Fluconazole) is INCORRECT — oral candidiasis plaques wipe off; leukoplakia does NOT wipe off. This is the critical distinction.

Option C (Topical aciclovir) is INCORRECT — leukoplakia is not viral.

Option D (Reassure + smoking cessation) is INCORRECT — a 3-month delay is dangerous with a red-flag lesion present. Smoking cessation is important but does not replace biopsy.

Option E (Nystatin) is for candidiasis — wrong diagnosis.

Clinical pearl: Absence of pain does NOT exclude oral cancer. Non-wipeable white patch + >3 weeks + tobacco/alcohol = 2WW regardless of symptoms.
